quotes.quote: After the first detection of a long monotonous change of the optical polarisation plane - nowadays termed as ``rotation'' or ``swing'' -- the attention of the community was caught when a similar event was found in the blazar BL Lacertae . In that particular case the rotation appeared to be associated with structural evolution of the radio jet as well as flaring activity at high energies. The rotation of the optical polarisation plane was interpreted as the natural consequence of an emission element following a helical path. The sequence of events observed at the source (structural evolution, optical-to-gamma rays outburst, and delayed radio flaring activity) led to the understanding that the region where the acceleration takes place is dominated by a helical magnetic field thus providing insight on the inner parts of the jet.

sources.summary: The paper studies BL Lacertae with three years of KVN radio data during two gamma-ray outbursts, emphasizing its variable blazar/AGN jet and finding no obvious radio counterpart.

quotes.quote: BL Lacertae (redshift z=0.069 , image scale 1.29 pc/mas), the prototypical BL Lac object, is characterized by strong flux variability from radio to rays, high radio brightness ( > 1 Jy), superluminal motion of jet components, and linearly polarized emission.

We analyzed multi-frequency KVN radio data of BL Lac spanning three years, from January 2013 to March 2016 to study the connection between radio and ray emission in blazar jets. We obtained light curves and spectra of the radio core at mm-wavelengths during a period when BL Lac showed two ray outbursts that occurred in November 2013 and March 2015.
There is no obvious radio counterpart to any of the two ray outbursts covered by our study.

sources.summary: The paper treats BL Lac as a source with year-long optical and gamma-ray quasi-periodicity, included among three sources with moderate but non-negligible significance.

quotes.quote: We found Sandrinelli2016b,Sandrinelli2017 year-long possible ray and optical correlated quasi-periodicities in PKS0537 441 (z=0.892) and BL Lac (z=0.069), corresponding to 0.77 yr and 1.86 yr, respectively.

Considering also the results for BL Lac see and Table S Sandrinelli2017 , the main conclusion of our analysis is that the three sources reveal quasi-periodicities with a moderate yet non-negligible statistical significance. The quasi-periodicities occur at the same frequency in both the optical and rays for BL Lac and PG 1553+113, and at correlated frequencies for PKS2155 304. We note that the peak significances obtained with the procedure adopted in this paper appear modest in comparison with those proposed in the literature and quoted in Section theintro . In this paper we have reconsidered the search for year-long quasi-periodicities in optical and rays of the two BL Lac sources PKS2155 304 and PG1553+113, using a procedure adapted from the recipes of and described in our paper on BL Lac Sandrinelli2017 .

sources.summary: The paper reports multiband monitoring of BL Lacertae, finding non-achromatic optical flux variability, bluer-when-brighter behavior, a 0.6 mag B-band night-to-night change, and contrasting spectral hardening relative to S5 0716+714.

quotes.quote: On the other hand, our essentially simultaneous multiband optical observations of BL Lacertae in October 2001 showed flux variations that were not achromatic. This blazar definitely was found to become bluer when brighter on intra-night time scales and there is a less significant trend of the same type on inter-night time scales. Based on five nights of observations during a week, BL Lacertae showed a peak night-to-night variability of 0.6 mag in B . Thus, we found that the present optical observations of the two prominent blazars, made with similarly high sensitivity, reveal a contrasting behaviour in terms of the dependence of spectral hardening with increasing brightness, at least on intra-night, and possibly also on inter-night, time scales.

sources.summary: The paper uses BL Lac components to compare RRFID and 2 cm VLBA proper motions, finding that they drive one correlation but are not required after discrepant components are removed.

quotes.quote: The proper motions from the two surveys show a correlation with high significance (linear correlation coefficient of 0.81); however, this correlation is mainly due to the similar measurements for the proper motions of three components in BL Lac, which lie in the upper right corner corner of the figure. If those three components are excluded, then there is no statistically significant correlation between the proper motions measured by the two surveys. This lack of correlation is caused by the presence of a non-negligible subset of components with systematic differences between their proper motion measurements in the two surveys --- this subset of components is identified and discussed in detail below. If that subset of 12 components is removed from Figure 9, then a correlation with high significance is recovered, even when the three components of BL Lac are excluded (linear correlation coefficient of 0.76).

sources.summary: BL Lacertae showed a positive color-magnitude correlation with substantial scatter, significant flux changes, and optical spectral variation from about 2 to 3.

quotes.quote: BL Lacertae: The source was observed during 65 nights in 2008, 2009, and 2012. A positive CM correlation can be seen in the entire dataset, albeit with a large scatter in agreement with Villata2002,Villata2004,Ikejiri2011 . The observed flux changes were significant ( 1.5 mag), and the optical spectrum varied from _ BR 2 up to 3.

sources.summary: The paper measures a small 22-43 GHz core shift in the BL Lacertae jet and argues it supports a millimeter VLBI core associated with a recollimation shock.

quotes.quote: The result is an astrometric calibration and the measurement of the core-shift between 22 and 43 GHz for the jet in BL Lacertae to be -8 5, 20 6 as, in RA and Declination, respectively. By comparison to conventional phase referencing at cm-wavelengths we are able to show that this core shift at mm-wavelengths is significantly less than what would be predicted by extrapolating the low frequency result, which closely followed the predictions of the Blandford K onigl conical jet model. As such it would be the first demonstration for the association of the VLBI core with a recollimation shock, normally hidden at low frequencies due to the optical depth, which could be responsible for the ray production in blazar jets.

sources.summary: The paper treats BL Lac as a blazar with fast EVPA swings whose observed rotations and sky-plane jet oscillations agree with the proposed structural-change model.

quotes.quote: Other interesting source is BL Lac, which is observed to show fast EVPA swing events ( +210^ , 1993ApSS.206...55S ; +220^ , 2008Natur.452..966M ; 253^ , 2015MNRAS.453.1669B ), examples of which are presented in Fig. fig_bllac_mars . Observed behavior of these EVPA rotations is very close to the predictions of the proposed model (Fig. Piofphij ), suggesting structural changes in the jet of BL Lac. Indeed, 2003MNRAS.341..405S and 2013MNRAS.428..280C show that the BL Lac jet oscillates on the plane of the sky (see Fig. fig_pam ), which supports our assumptions.

sources.summary: The paper finds 2200+420 periodic at all monitored radio frequencies, with period estimates consistent with each other and optical results, though high-frequency residuals show an added impulsive signal.

quotes.quote: Periodicity is detected, by both the STIMA and the Lomb's methods, in light curves of 2200+420 at all frequencies (Fig. A2200_1 ), and period estimates are all consistent with each other , and close to the value obtained in the optical: 7.8 0.2 yr, i.e. 2849 73 days (Marchenko et al. 1996; Hagen-Thorn et al. 1997) . The periodicity is again most evident at 4.8 and 8 GHz, while at 22 and 37 GHz the residuals show the presence of an additional impulsive signal. At 14.5 GHz, the evidence of periodicity is marginal, although the estimated period is very close to that found in the other bands.

gmw_00000628.name: PGC421615
gmw_00000628.overview: PGC421615 is a relatively nearby host galaxy with a measured redshift of z=0.054 [arXiv:1605.01607]. Its spectrum exhibits emission lines of [OII], hydrogen, and [SII], and it has hosted a possible supernova located near its center [arXiv:1605.01607].

sources.summary: The paper identifies PGC421615 as the nearby host galaxy of a possible supernova, places the transient near its center, images the host field, and measures a redshift.

quotes.quote: This OT was classified as a PSN, being located 0.9"W and 3.6"N of the center of the galaxy PGC421615 (Btot=18.4m). The discovery and reference images are shown in Figure MASTER OTJ070747.72 . In order to study this supernova and its host galaxy in more detail, we took deep photometric images in B, g', ', i' and z' images of the area on 3 March 2016 with the SALTICAM CCD camera of the 10.4-meter Southern African Large Telescope (SALT) at the SAAO, as part of a spectroscopic followup program of MASTER OTs Buckley2016 .

On 10 March 2016, we obtained low resolution ( 300) spectrum, covering 340-1000nm, of the host galaxy PGC421615 in a 1800 s exposure. The spectrum, shown in Figure spec , has identified emission lines of [ OII 3727 ] , H and [ S II ] , resulting in a redshift determination of z=0.054, implying a relatively close galaxy.

gmw_00000629.name: NGC 4608
gmw_00000629.overview: NGC 4608 is a massive, strongly barred early-type lenticular galaxy located in the Virgo Cluster [arXiv:2101.05321]. It features a long, thin bar [arXiv:1405.6726] and a fainter inner disk characterized by conspicuous negative residual crescents on both sides of the bar [arXiv:0708.3870].

sources.summary: The paper finds conspicuous negative residual crescents in NGC 4608 and shows its inner disc is fainter than a normal model, with a Freeman type II disc improving the fit.

quotes.quote: Inspecting the results in Fig. results , it is possible to identify two particular cases where such negative residuals are not only conspicuous, but also clearly delineate a distinct region in the residual image. These are NGC 4608 and NGC 5701. In their residual images, one is able to spot a region in the disc where the models are definitely brighter than the galaxies. It can be described as two crescents, one at each side of the bar, but out of it (pointed out by the red arrows in Fig. results ).

The fainter, inner part of the disc in NGC 4608 can easily be identified in this figure, as a depression between 20 and 50 . One sees that, at the minimum of this depression, the model with a normal, type I disc is almost a factor of two brighter than the actual disc. On the other hand, using the model with a hole in the disc does not result in a better fit, since in this case one sees now a significant positive residual between 20 and the truncation radius. In the fit with a type II disc, however, the match between model and galaxy is clearly improved.

sources.summary: The paper decomposes NGC 4608 as a massive barred early-type disk galaxy with a B/P bar region and evidence for a modest, slowly rotating classical bulge.

quotes.quote: NGC 4608 and NGC 4643 are both massive, strongly barred, early-type disc galaxies. The former is located in the Virgo Cluster at a distance of 17.3 0.8 Mpc surface-brightness-fluctuation distance; cantiello18 ; the latter is in the nearby field (approximately 2.4 Mpc away from NGC 4608), with a redshift-based distance estimate of 19.3 2.9 Mpc. Assuming H_ 0 = 72 kpc ^ 1 and a Virgocentric-infall-corrected redshift of 1392 from HyperLEDA, along with an assumed uncertainty of 15 . These distances give scales of 83.9 pc/ for NGC 4608 and 93.6 pc/ for NGC 4643. NGC 4608 is classified in rc3 as SB0 and NGC 4643 as SB0/a; very faint, wispy spiral arms account for the latter classification e.g., erwin-sparke03 .

Our final model for NGC 4608 uses the combination of 's BrokenExponential component and the new GaussianRingAz component (Appendix app:ring ) for the disc and the inner ring surrounding the bar; the new FlatBar component for the outer part of the bar and a mildly elliptical Sersic GenEllipse component for the inner, B/P part of the bar; a central round S ersic component; and a compact, circular Gaussian. Table tab:n4608-decomp summarizes the best-fitting parameter values, while Table tab:fluxes shows the luminosities and relative fluxes of the different components, and Figures fig:n4608-bestfit-irac1 and fig:n4608-bestfit-f160w compare this model with the IRAC1 and F160W data.

sources.summary: The paper compares NGC 4608 with simulations, emphasizing its long thin bar relative to barlens and ring, decomposition against gtr115, and SB0/no-HI status explaining absent outer spiral structure.

quotes.quote: The second row from the top compares the image of the t =6 Gyr snapshot of gtr116 with the NIRS0S image of NGC 4608. In this case, the relevant extent of the thin part of the bar -- relative to both the extent of the barlens and the size of the inner ring -- is much longer than in the previous example. Note also how thin in the equatorial plane the part of the bar outside the barlens is in the (near-)face-on views of both the galaxy and the simulation snapshot.
Fig. fig:comp-NGC5101_gtr115 compares the decomposition results for NGC 4608 from the NIRS0S sample and for simulation gtr115.

Note also that the spirals in the outer parts of the simulation are not present in the galaxy image. This can be understood because the simulation has at the time of the comparison still nearly 6 of its baryons in the form of gas, which NGC 4608, being a SB0 without HI detection, does not have.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 4608 as a barred face-on S0 whose decomposition shows no relevant disk, motivating secular-evolution or disk-free bar-formation explanations.

quotes.quote: We have applied a 2D structural analysis code developed by des97 to the optical and near-infrared images of the SB0 face--on galaxies NGC 4608 and NGC 5701. The code performs a bulge/disk decomposition and has been tested and applied in a hundred galaxies (see des02 ). The results of this analysis indicate that these galaxies do not have relevant disks; the major component is indeed the bulge (see Fig. 1). Then, one is left with two possibilities. Either the disks in these galaxies were almost completely destroyed by strong secular evolution processes, or these galaxies have never had disks, which requires a new mechanism for bar formation other than the disk instability.

On the other hand, if secular evolution is the answer to explain the absence of a disk in NGC 4608 and NGC 5701, then these are good examples of how strong these processes may be and how seriously they should be considered.

sources.summary: The paper places NGC 4608 in the evolved-bar bin: bulge and bar light may depress _z, but high bar dispersion and radial profiles support an evolved bar.

quotes.quote: NGC 4608 and NGC 5701 are similar cases. The bulge and bar influence may be causing low values for _z , but the high values of _ z, bar clearly put these bars in the evolved bin. Their bulges are, however, not as dynamically hot as those of NGC 1387 and NGC 1440 and their _z radial profiles resemble Fig. 6(d).

gmw_00000630.name: NGC 5701
gmw_00000630.overview: NGC 5701 is a nearly face-on S0/a galaxy [arXiv:1610.02330] featuring a weak bar [arXiv:0808.1841] and a barlens dominated by an old stellar population [arXiv:1609.01936]. It is surrounded by a detached star-forming ring [arXiv:0908.3564] and a large outer ring of cold dust [arXiv:1507.08778].

sources.summary: The paper finds NGC 5701 has extended negative residual crescents between its bar and outer ring, making its inner disc much fainter than the model and requiring more complex modelling.

quotes.quote: Inspecting the results in Fig. results , it is possible to identify two particular cases where such negative residuals are not only conspicuous, but also clearly delineate a distinct region in the residual image. These are NGC 4608 and NGC 5701. In their residual images, one is able to spot a region in the disc where the models are definitely brighter than the galaxies. It can be described as two crescents, one at each side of the bar, but out of it (pointed out by the red arrows in Fig. results ).

A possible explanation for this difference is that in NGC 4608 the two crescents together form a well defined single region with negative residuals, whereas in NGC 5701 the two crescents are more detached from each other, resulting in two separate regions with negative residuals. Thus, it seems that a more complex model is necessary. In Figs. results and resp one sees that the mismatch between galaxy and model in the inner disc of this galaxy is even more accentuated than in the case of NGC 4608. The area of the disc in NGC 5701 between the bar and the outer ring is about 2-3 times fainter than the model.

sources.summary: The paper uses NGC 5701 as a barlens comparison object, describing its group membership, spectroscopy coverage, flat colors, old stellar populations, and metallicity behavior across the barlens.

quotes.quote: NGC 5701 [ (R'1)SA B (rl,bl)0/a ] : This galaxy belongs to our barlens group b (Fig. fig:obs_groups1 ) and parent galaxy group 2 (Fig. fig:obs_parents ). Integral-field spectroscopy was made by Seidel et al. (2015) using a field-of-view of 36'', which covers most of the barlens size having a radius = 25'' in our definition. We use this radius to evaluate the stellar population measurements by Seidel et al. The optical colors are studied by HE+2016: this galaxy shows flat (g-) and (i-z) color profiles throughout the bar major axis, which colors are also typical for the elliptical galaxies.

Using full spectral fitting Seidel et al. find flat (old) age and metallicity profiles in the barlens region (their Fig. 6). Deviations from that appear only in the innermost 4'', where nuclear spiral arms reside erwin2002 , manifested also as a sigma-drop in the same region. Seidel et al. divide the stars also into three sub-populations with different stellar ages (their Fig. 8). It appears that the mass of the barlens is dominated by the old stellar population (70 -- 85 of the mass have ages > 10 Gyr).

sources.summary: The paper calls NGC5701 the bluest transition-region object and a possible Hoag-type barred galaxy with a detached star-forming ring whose origin may involve satellite accretion or bar instability.

quotes.quote: NGC5701 is the bluest object in the transition region. We classified this galaxy as an Sa (NED gives S0/a). However its peculiar morphology, characterized by a central bulge surrounded by a faint and apparently detached star-forming ring, makes NGC5701 a possible Hoag-type object hoag50 . In this case the central condensation is a bar, not a `bulge or elliptical' hoag50 and dubbed this object `a barred galaxy without a disk'. The origin of Hoag-type objects is still a puzzle, but two different scenarii seem to be the most commonly accepted: a) accretion of small satellites schweizer87 , or b) a strong bar instability brosh85 , which destroys the disk and builds up a ring (e.g. schwarz84,byrd94b ).

The lack of detailed dynamical and spectroscopic data prevent us from driving a conclusion on the origin of the ring in NGC5701, but it is quite likely that the bar is playing an important role either destroying the disk or preventing the accreted gas from infalling into the center.

sources.summary: The paper describes NGC5701 as a cold-dust source with submillimetre and optical profiles that differ, especially a large cold-dust ring beyond most starlight.

quotes.quote: Two of the sources (NGC5701 and NGC5669) were observed as part of the Herschel Reference Survey (Boselli et al. 2010), and a third (NGC3049) was observed by the KINGFISH programme (Kennicutt et al. 2010). The SPIRE images were obtained from the Herschel or HerMES data archives. For each of these objects we used an SDSS i band optical image to trace the optical surface brightness profile from stellar emission after smoothing by a gaussian matched to the size of the 250 m SPIRE beam, and then compared this to the 250 m surface brightness profile resulting from dust emission.

In all cases the optical and submm surface brightness profiles are clearly different. This is most obvious in NGC5701, where there is a large ring of cold dust located outside most of the optical light distribution (see Fig. 6R).
This ring makes a substantial contribution to the integrated submm emission, but the optical emission from starlight in this region is much less than in the more central regions of this object.

sources.summary: The paper treats NGC 5701 as a barred face-on S0 whose decomposition shows no relevant disk, motivating secular-evolution or disk-free bar-formation explanations.

quotes.quote: We have applied a 2D structural analysis code developed by des97 to the optical and near-infrared images of the SB0 face--on galaxies NGC 4608 and NGC 5701. The code performs a bulge/disk decomposition and has been tested and applied in a hundred galaxies (see des02 ). The results of this analysis indicate that these galaxies do not have relevant disks; the major component is indeed the bulge (see Fig. 1). Then, one is left with two possibilities. Either the disks in these galaxies were almost completely destroyed by strong secular evolution processes, or these galaxies have never had disks, which requires a new mechanism for bar formation other than the disk instability.

On the other hand, if secular evolution is the answer to explain the absence of a disk in NGC 4608 and NGC 5701, then these are good examples of how strong these processes may be and how seriously they should be considered.

sources.summary: The paper compares NGC 5701 to simulations of R1R2' morphology, describing it as a weak-bar galaxy with a strong R1 ring, weaker R2 ring, and slower ring alignment.

quotes.quote: From top to bottom Figure b2var.eps shows NGC 5701, and NGC 5101. Figure stbar.eps compares NGC 6782 and NGC 3504 with density distributions showing simulations that have stabilized and exhibit R1R2 morphology. NGC 5701 is a fairly weak bar with Q_T=0.14 that shows a strong R1 ring but a weaker R2 ring. NGC 5701 is compared to a simulation one period after bar growth has completed. At this time the spiral arms generated during bar growth are beginning to close and will soon form both R1 and R2 rings. As NGC 5701 has a weaker bar it may take longer for R1 and R2 rings to form and become aligned perpendicular and parallel with the bar, respectively.

sources.summary: The paper classifies NGC 5701 as a nearly face-on S0/a barlens galaxy whose barlens shows flat optical color profiles and normal elliptical-galaxy-like colors.

quotes.quote: NGC 5701 (F): is an S0/a galaxy, at an inclination of inc = 15.2 ^ . The color profiles both along the bar major and minor axes are completely flat, and the barlens shows no structure in the optical colors. The median colors are (g-) 0.77 and (i-z) 0.27, which are also normal colors of elliptical galaxies according to shimasaku2001 .

sources.summary: The paper places NGC 5701 in the evolved-bar bin: bulge and bar light may depress _z, but high bar dispersion and radial profiles support an evolved bar.

quotes.quote: NGC 4608 and NGC 5701 are similar cases. The bulge and bar influence may be causing low values for _z , but the high values of _ z, bar clearly put these bars in the evolved bin. Their bulges are, however, not as dynamically hot as those of NGC 1387 and NGC 1440 and their _z radial profiles resemble Fig. 6(d).

gmw_00000631.name: Eridanus II
gmw_00000631.overview: Eridanus II is an ultra-faint dwarf galaxy [arXiv:2109.01177] located near the Milky Way's virial radius [arXiv:2109.01177, arXiv:2403.19015] and is likely on its first infall into the system [arXiv:2111.00045]. It is the lowest-luminosity galaxy known to host a central star cluster [arXiv:2403.19015], which has been used to constrain various dark matter models [arXiv:1810.08543, arXiv:2403.19015].

sources.summary: The paper studies Eri II as an ultra-faint dwarf, deriving its RR Lyrae distance, metallicity spread, stellar population gradients, and structural parameters.

quotes.quote: We present a detailed study of the variable star population of Eridanus II (Eri II), an ultra-faint dwarf galaxy that lies close to the Milky Way virial radius. We analyze multi-epoch g,,i ground-based data from Goodman and the Dark Energy Camera, plus F475W, F606W, F814W space data from the Advanced Camera for Surveys. We report the detection of 67 RR Lyrae (RRL) stars and 2 Anomalous Cepheids, most of them new discoveries. With the RRL stars, we measure the distance modulus of Eri II, _0=22.84 0.05  mag (D _ =370 9  kpc) and derive a metallicity spread of 0.3 dex (0.2 dex intrinsic).

The more metal-rich RRL and the RRab stars have greater central concentration than the more metal-poor RRL and the RRc stars that are mainly located outside 1   _ h . This is similar to what is found in larger dwarf galaxies such as Sculptor, and in agreement with an outside-in galaxy formation scenario. This is remarkable in such a faint dwarf galaxy with an apparently single and extremely short ( <1  Gyr) star formation burst. Finally, we have derived new and independent structural parameters for Eri II and its star cluster using our new data that are in very good agreement with previous estimates.

sources.summary: The paper constructs Eridanus II’s HST CaHK metallicity distribution, finds a leaky-box-like MDF, updates its orbit, and argues for an isolated-galaxy-like evolutionary history.

quotes.quote: Sandford Department of Astronomy, University of California, Berkeley, Berkeley, CA, 94720, USA We use deep narrowband Ca H K ( F395N ) imaging taken with the Hubble Space Telescope (HST) to construct the metallicity distribution function (MDF) of Local Group (LG) ultra-faint dwarf (UFD) galaxy Eridanus II (Eri II ). When combined with archival F475W and F814W data, we measure metallicities for 60 resolved red giant branch stars as faint as m_ F475W 24 mag, a factor of 4 x more stars than current spectroscopic MDF determinations. We find that Eri II has a mean metallicity of [ Fe/H ] = ^ + _ and a dispersion of _ [ Fe/H ] = ^ + _ , which are consistent with spectroscopic MDFs, though more precisely constrained owing to a larger sample. We identify a handful of extremely metal-poor star candidates (EMP; [ Fe/H ] < -3 ) that are marginally bright enough for spectroscopic follow up. Eri II 's MDF appears well-described by a leaky box chemical evolution model.

We also compute an updated orbital history for Eri II using Gaia eDR3 proper motions, and find that it is likely on first infall into the Milky Way. Our findings suggest that Eri II underwent an evolutionary history similar to that of an isolated galaxy. Compared to MDFs for select cosmological simulations of similar mass galaxies, we find that Eri II has a lower fraction of stars with [ Fe/H ] < -3 , though such comparisons should currently be treated with caution due to a paucity of simulations, selection effects, and known limitations of Ca H K for EMPs.

sources.summary: The paper models Eridanus II as an ultra-faint dwarf whose offset star cluster is best explained by a low-density dark matter core; partial-core tidal destruction is disfavored by expected tails.

quotes.quote: The Eridanus II (EriII) `ultra-faint' dwarf has a large ( 15 pc) and low mass ( 4.3 10^3 M _ ) star cluster (SC) offset from its centre by 23 3 pc in projection. Its size and offset are naturally explained if EriII has a central dark matter core, but such a core may be challenging to explain in a CDM cosmology. In this paper, we revisit the survival and evolution of EriII's SC, focussing for the first time on its puzzingly large ellipticity ( 0.31^ +0.05 _ 0.06 ). We perform a suite of 960 direct N body simulations of SCs, orbiting within a range of spherical background potentials fit to ultra-faint dwarf galaxy simulations. We find only two scenarios that come close to explaining EriII's SC.

In the first, EriII has a low density dark matter core (of size 70 pc and density 2 10^8 M _ kpc ^ 3 ). In this model, the high ellipticity of EriII's SC is set at birth, with the lack of tidal forces in the core allowing its ellipticity to remain frozen in for long times. In the second, EriII's SC orbits in a partial core, with its high ellipticity owing to its imminent tidal destruction.

sources.summary: Eridanus II is an ultra-faint Milky Way satellite with no gas, measured member-star kinematics, and a faint central cluster whose survival probes its dark-matter halo profile.

quotes.quote: The recent detection of two faint and extended star clusters in the central regions of two Local Group dwarf galaxies, Eridanus II and Andromeda XXV, raises the question of whether clusters with such low densities can survive the tidal field of cold dark matter haloes with central density cusps. Using both analytic arguments and a suite of collisionless N-body simulations, I show that these clusters are extremely fragile and quickly disrupted in the presence of central cusps ^ with 0.2 . Furthermore, the scenario in which the clusters were originally more massive and sank to the center of the halo requires extreme fine tuning and does not naturally reproduce the observed systems. In turn, these clusters are long lived in cored haloes, whose central regions are safe shelters for 0.2 . The only viable scenario for hosts that have preserved their primoridal cusp to the present time is that the clusters formed at rest at the bottom of the potential, which is easily tested by measurement of the clusters proper velocity within the host. This offers means to readily probe the central density profile of two dwarf galaxies as faint as L_V 5 10^5 L_ and L_V 6 10^4 L_ , in which stellar feedback is unlikely to be effective.

At a distance of D 370 kpc, EriII is a UF satellite of the MW, with a luminosity of L_V 6 10^4 L_ , a projected half-light radius of R_h 280 pc, a quite high ellipticity 0.48 and no evidence for the presence of gas DC16 . Using Magellan/IMACS spectroscopy Li17 have recently targeted EriII and confirmed 28 member stars. They find a velocity dispersion of =6.9^ +1.2 _ 0.9 kms ^ 1 , a mean metallicity of Fe/H 2.4 , and can exclude the presence of young stars in the system. A round overdensity of stars near the center of EriII was already spotted in the discovery data SK15 , and subsequent deep imaging DC16 has confirmed the presence of an extremely faint and quite extended star cluster. This has a luminosity of L_ V, 2 10^3 L_ and a projected half-light radius of R_ h, 13 pc \ . As such, EriII is the least luminous galaxy known to posses a stellar cluster, which has prompted the suggestions that other distant Milky Way GCs might in fact be hosted by yet undiscovered low surface brightness galaxies DZ16 .

sources.summary: The paper uses Eri II's lone star cluster to probe its central dark matter profile, favoring a dark matter core because cored models preserve the observed cluster properties.

quotes.quote: As a first application, we apply our method to the ultra-faint dwarf galaxy Eridanus II (Eri II) that was recently discovered by the Dark Energy Survey (DES; Bechtol2015,Koposov2015 ). Eri II is situated 366 from the Sun, at the edge of the MW, with M_V=-7.1 , a half-light radius of R_ 1/2 = 2.31' , and an ellipticity of 0.48 . Eri II appears to show an extended star formation history, but follow-up observations are needed to confirm this. Koposov2015 and Crnojevic2016 found that Eri II has a lone star cluster at a projected distance 45 from Eri II's centre, with M_V=-3.5 and a half-light radius of 13 see Table 1 Crnojevic2016 . Compared to the MW's star clusters 2010 edition Harris1996 , Eri II's star cluster appears faint and extended, contributing just 4 of Eri II's total luminosity.

Our key result is that we favour a DM core over a cusp in the ultra-faint dwarf galaxy Eri II. In models with a DM cusp, Eri II's star cluster is rapidly destroyed by tides, whereas in cored models the star cluster survives for more than a Hubble time, naturally reaching an asymptotic size and mass consistent with observations.

sources.summary: The paper discusses Eridanus II as a dwarf galaxy whose central compact star cluster constrains axion dark matter soliton models and may be stabilized in two-component scenarios.

quotes.quote: On the other front, a central compact star cluster has been observed in the dwarf galaxy Eridanus II . In the ADM context, the star cluster should sit inside a soliton, one of the unique nonlinear features of the ADM. However, the soliton random walk is prone to destroy the observed central star cluster; although one solution has been proposed in , their simulation has imposed a 5 Gyr bound on the star cluster's age. Two-component universes may provide a different scenario where the star cluster is strongly trapped by a second more compact soliton.

Two-component models offer a possibility of coexisting solitons of different sizes at the centers of galaxies . The soliton within another soliton scenario may solve the age problem of the star cluster at the center of the dwarf galaxy (Eridanus II) . However, due to the non-linearity nature of the soliton, further simulations for this scenario are needed.

sources.summary: The paper uses Eridanus II's dark-matter-dominated mass profile and central star cluster survival to revise fuzzy dark matter constraints from soliton oscillations.

quotes.quote: A recent investigation into the survival of the ancient central star cluster (SC) of the ultra-faint dwarf galaxy Eridanus II (Eri II) by Marsh Niemeyer (2019) , henceforth abbreviated as , estimated the gravitational heating effect caused by stochastic fluctuations and soliton density oscillations. By approximating the dynamical timescales of both mechanisms, a lower bound m_b 10^ 19 eV (with partial exclusions in the range 10^ 20 < m_b < 10^ 19 eV) was derived together with the constraint from the subhalo mass function. This bound is rather stringent compared with other constraints from, for example, Lyman- forest m_b 2 10^ 21 eV , DM (sub)halo abundance m_b 2 3 10^ 21 , galaxy luminosity function at high redshifts m_b 1 8 10^ 22 , and heating of galactic disks m_b 1 2 10^ 22 . In this work, we present a theoretical framework that pins down the dynamical timescales of both the ground-state soliton wavefunction _ 00 and the coherent density oscillations _ soliton for a perturbed FDM soliton. Concerning the stability of Eri II SC, demonstrates that the SC undergoes a complete tidal disruption within 1 due to soliton random walk excursions in the presence of a background halo; the effect can be largely mitigated if the background halo is stripped away by the Milky Way tides. Observational constraints on Eri II indicate that the mass contribution within its half-light radius is predominantly sourced by the soliton core for m_b 10^ 21 eV. We confirm both analytically and with N-body and FDM simulations that the effect of gravitational heating on Eri II SC due to soliton density oscillations is negligible for m_b 4 10^ 21 , contrary to the conclusion drawn by .

Constraints from Eridanus II The half-light radius of Eri II is estimated to be r_ EII =277 14 pc , within which the mass enclosed is M_ EII ^ r_ EII =1.2^ +0.4 _ 0.3 10^7 M _ , corresponding to an average total mass density _ EII 0.135 M _ pc ^ 3 . The inferred mass-to-light ratio indicates Eri II is DM-dominated . The central SC of Eri II appears to be an intermediate-age population ( 3 13 Gyr) with a half-light radius of r_ SC =13 1 pc and a mass of M_ SC ^ r_ SC 2000 M _ (assuming that the stellar mass-to-light ratio is unity) .

sources.summary: The paper reports a Gaia-anchored Eridanus II distance lower than a prior result, contrasts the methodologies, and notes unresolved tension among published larger and smaller distances.

quotes.quote: Using the same data, we find =22.67 0.04 for Eridanus II, which is 0.2 mag lower than the distance of =22.84 0.05 reported by martinez-vazquez_monelli_cassisi_et_al_2021 . The two studies adopt the same metallicity ( [ Fe/H ] =-2.4 ); li2017 ), though use very different methodologies to derive the distance. Whereas we use the PWZ, martinez-vazquez_monelli_cassisi_et_al_2021 using the I-band PLZ along with a calibration ultimately tied to Hipparcos . It's unclear that this is sufficient to account for a 0.2 mag difference. Literature distances to Eridanus II span a wide range that are consistent with both values.

There are a set of larger distances ( =22.8-22.9 ; koposov2015 , crnojevic2016 , gallart2021 ) and smaller distances ( =22.6-22.7 ; bechtol2015 , simon2021 ). At present, the primary reason for the tension between the two groups of distance determinations is unclear.

sources.summary: The paper models Eridanus II as an ultra-faint dwarf galaxy with a central star cluster, using its dark-matter halo and stellar dynamics to constrain primordial-black-hole dark matter.

quotes.quote: We focus on the Eridanus II (Eri II) system given that, compared to the data used in , novel and critical observational data are now available on this system and on its central dense stellar cluster, in particular from Refs. . Eri II is a dwarf galaxy located in the vicinity of the Milky Way, just beyond our Galaxy's virial radius. It resides at a distance of D = 366 17 kpc , with a half-light radius of R_ h = 277 14 pc and houses a star cluster near its center with a half-light radius of r_ h = 13 1 pc . The dwarf galaxy was discovered relatively late in 2015 by the Dark Energy Survey (DES) and is classified as one of the Milky Way's satellite galaxies. The late discovery can likely be attributed to its relatively low luminosity; at an absolute magnitude of M_V = -7.1 , Eri II is one of the least luminous galaxies known to contain a central star cluster .

We use data from Zoutendijk et al. to describe the DM halo of the Eri II galaxy. Zoutendijk et al. employ stellar line-of-sight velocities gathered from observations of Eri II during the MUSE (Multi Unit Spectroscopic Explorer on the Very Large Telescope)-Faint survey to derive constraints on the DM profile.

sources.summary: The paper treats Eri II as the most distant new Milky Way satellite galaxy candidate, finds no associated H I, and notes possible recent star formation and gas below the reported upper limit.

quotes.quote: Three of the satellite candidates lie beyond 100 kpc , raising the question of whether they may still contain some neutral gas. This could in particular be the case for the most distant object, Eri II, which possibly contains a younger stellar population Koposov2015 .

Interestingly, Koposov2015 report possible evidence for more recent star formation in the most distant galaxy candidate, Eri II, consistent with its current location just outside the virial radius of the Milky Way. Their finding suggests that some H i gas may still be present in Eri II, although below the upper limit of about 2.5 10^ 4 M_ reported here.

We do not detect any H i signal likely to be associated with any of the nine galaxy candidates, including the most distant object, Eri II. The resulting upper H i mass limits are typically in the range of several hundred to a few thousand solar masses, with a slightly higher limit of about 2.5 10^ 4 M_ for Eri II.

sources.summary: The paper uses Eridanus II's central star cluster and ultra-faint dwarf properties to constrain MACHO dark matter, arguing cluster survival excludes substantial compact-object dark matter in key mass ranges.

quotes.quote: I show that a recently discovered star cluster near the center of the ultra-faint dwarf galaxy Eridanus II provides strong constraints on massive compact halo objects (MACHOs) of 5 M_ as the main component of dark matter. MACHO dark matter will dynamically heat the cluster, driving it to larger sizes and higher velocity dispersions until it dissolves into its host galaxy. Both Eri II's cluster and the compact ultra-faint dwarfs are characterized by stellar masses of just a few thousand M_ and half-light radii of 13 pc (for the cluster) and 30 pc (for the ultra-faint dwarfs).

Eridanus II was discovered as part of the Dark Energy Survey Koposov+Belokurov+Torrealba+etal_2015,Bechtol+Drlica-Wagner+Balbinot+etal_2015 . It has an absolute magnitude of M_V = -7.1 and a half-light radius of almost 300 pc, and lies just beyond the Galaxy's virial radius at a distance of 366 17 kpc Crnojevic+Sand+Zaritsky_2016 . Eri II hosts a single star cluster of absolute magnitude M_V = -3.5 ( 2000 L_ ,V ) and half-light radius r_h = 13 pc.

sources.summary: The paper uses Eridanus II's central star cluster, mass, velocity dispersion, and dark-matter density to constrain fuzzy dark matter, arguing that formation and cluster survival disfavor wide axion-mass ranges.

quotes.quote: Eridanus II (Eri II) is a UFD with a centrally located star cluster . Eri II is located at a distance of 370 kpc from the centre of the MW. The mass within the half-light radius is estimated as M_ EII =1.2^ +0.4 _ 0.3 10^7 M_ , 1D velocity dispersion _v=6.9^ +1.2 _ 0.9 km s ^ 1 , and central DM density _ DM =0.15 M_ pc ^ 3 . The central star cluster has a half light radius r_h=13 pc , age T_ EII =3 12 Gyr and mass M_ =2000 M_ .

The formation of Eri II as a subhalo demands m_a 0.8 10^ 21 eV if FDM is all of the DM. The range 0.8 10^ 21 eV m_a 10^ 19 eV is further disfavoured by the observed size and age of the Eri II star cluster.

sources.summary: The paper finds Eridanus II favors a cusped dark matter density profile that remains robust to wider priors, with its inner kinematic sample driving the constraint.

quotes.quote: The inner slope parameter of a dark matter density profile also shows a wide spread within its prior range. Nonetheless, several UFDs (Eridanus II, Segue 1, and Willman 1) favor cusped dark matter density profiles.

Firstly, Segue 1 and Willman 1 favor a sharply cusped inner slope of their dark matter density profiles ( 1.6 for Segue 1 and 1.2 for Willman 1), even considering 95 confidence intervals of the profile. Eridanus II also prefers a cusped dark matter density profile ( 1.2 ), even though the uncertainty for its is larger than for Segue 1 and Willman 1. Intriguingly, the slope of Segue 1 is much steeper than an NFW cusp ( =1 ) but is roughly consistent with ones predicted by the other simulation works e.g., 1997ApJ...477L...9F,1999MNRAS.310.1147M,2008MNRAS.391.1685S,2020MNRAS.492.3662I , while those of Eridanus II and Willman 1 could advocate the NFW ones.

On the other hand, the galaxies having a cusped dark matter halo like Eridanus II, Segue 1 and Willman 1 do not change so much their dark matter density profiles even for the new prior range. The possible reason why these galaxies prefer cusped dark matter density profiles comes from the following properties of their line-of-sight velocity dispersion profile in the central region. Although the variations of Q (dark halo shape) and _z (velocity anisotropy) give a similar effect on entire shape of line-of-sight velocity dispersion profiles see 2008MNRAS.390...71C,2015ApJ...810...22H , the inner slope of dark matter density profile, , can have an impact only on a central velocity dispersion profile 2020ApJ...904...45H . Note that cusped dark matter density profiles in Eridanus II, Segue 1 and Willman 1 come mainly from the kinematic sample in the inner part (especially within 10 pc) in our unbinned analysis.

sources.summary: The paper discusses Eridanus II as the lowest-mass known galaxy hosting one globular cluster, with a low-mass GC, roughly 10^9 solar-mass halo, and cuspy dark-matter profile.

quotes.quote: Presently, Eridanus II stands as the galaxy with the lowest known mass hosting a single GC. Remarkably, it aligns well with the GC mass--halo mass relationship, as its lone GC possesses a mass of M_ GC 4 10^3  , an extremely low value compared to the general population of GCs. The estimated halo mass of Eridanus II is around 10^9  . Since the GC in Eridanus II sits at the lower end of the GC mass function and fits the GC mass --halo mass relation, this may indicate that smaller GCs may better withstand the conditions in lower mass galaxies. Additionally, reveal that the best-fitting dark matter halo profile is not cored, but rather peaked or ``cuspy.''
Eridanus II, originally discovered by the Dark Energy Survey, has an estimated stellar mass of approximately 5.9 10^4  , with its GC near the center weighing in at 4 10^3  Crnojevic2016 .

sources.summary: Eridanus II is described as a very low-luminosity galaxy with a faint central globular cluster and an old, metal-poor stellar population confirmed spectroscopically.

quotes.quote: Eridanus II 2016ApJ...824L..14C measured the properties of a faint GC ( M_V = --3.5) near the centre of the very low luminosity ( M_V = --7.1) galaxy Eridanus II. This single GC has a corresponding mass of 4.0 10^3 M _ . The GC has a CMD that is similar to its host galaxy, i.e. dominated by an old ( 10 Gyr) and metal-poor ( [ Fe/H ] -2.5) stellar population. 2017ApJ...838....8L confirmed the low metallicity finding [ Fe/H ] = --2.38 and measured a velocity dispersion of 6.9 km/s.

sources.summary: The review treats Eridanus II as a spectroscopically confirmed ultra-faint dwarf near the luminosity boundary, notable for containing a star cluster useful for dark-matter constraints.

quotes.quote: Among the post-2005 discoveries, only four galaxies are within 1 magnitude of this boundary: CVn I ( M_ V = -8.7 ), Crater II ( M_ V = -8.2 ), Leo T ( M_ V = -8.0 ), and Eridanus II ( M_ V = -7.2 ). The first three of these systems stand out from the fainter population in obvious ways: CVn I is substantially more luminous, larger, and more metal-rich e.g., martin07,martin08,sg07,munoz18b , Crater II is a factor of 4 more extended than any fainter dwarf torrealba16 , and Leo T hosts neutral gas and recent star formation rw08,dejong08 . These objects more closely resemble the previously-known dSphs and dSph/dIrrs in the Local Group. Eridanus II, on the other hand, is distinct from other UFDs only in that it contains a star cluster crnojevic16b . Setting the dividing line such that it lands between Eridanus II and Leo T is therefore sensible, and minimizes the likelihood that future revisions to the absolute magnitudes of any of these systems will blur the boundary.

For example, brandt16 used the presence of a star cluster in Eridanus II to place tight constraints on MACHO dark matter, and penarrubia16 proposed that wide binary stars may be disrupted by the dark matter potential of a UFD, allowing a measurement of the dark matter density profile.

sources.summary: The paper treats Eridanus II as an ultrafaint dwarf galaxy whose central star cluster can constrain fuzzy dark matter through halo-driven cluster expansion, with caveats about solitonic-core assumptions.

quotes.quote: In Section  sec:comparison we compare the resulting constraints to those from other work. In that section, we also discuss the predictions of our present model regarding the expansion of the central star cluster of the dwarf galaxy Eridanus II, as studied in  by adopting the original formulation of  . The technical details associated with that discussion are given in Appendix  App:Eri .

As shown in Appendix  App:Eri , directly applying our extended and improved model to the dynamics central cluster of Eridanus II leads to the similarly strong constraint m 8.8 10^ 20 eV , given one assumes that the FDM makes up all the dark matter and the effect associated fluctuations goes entirely into expanding the cluster, in the manner envisaged by  . However, for masses m 10^ 20 eV the cluster should lie inside the solitonic core of the FDM distribution ( Marsh2018 ).

sources.summary: The paper includes Eridanus 2 among isolated Local Group dwarfs with existing Gaia-based proper motion, giving table coordinates and finding a Milky Way backsplash orbit is possible only at low tangential velocity.

quotes.quote: An additional three galaxies (Leo T, Eridanus 2 and Phoenix) are close enough that their proper motions have already been derived using red giant branch stars.

For the three closest galaxies (Eridanus 2, Phoenix and Leo T), the brightest red giant branch stars in these systems are luminous enough to have reliable astrometry in Gaia DR2, and proper motions for these three systems have previously been measured ( fritz2018, pace2019 ; hereafter MV2020 mcconnachie2020 ).

Eridanus 2: This dwarf can only have passed within 300 kpc of the Milky Way if its Galactocentric tangential velocity is less than 90 km s ^ 1 . This is at the 1- edge of the current proper motion measurement. The allowable parameter space for a backsplash solution is consistent with a similar study by , who consider the orbits of Eridanus 2, Phoenix, Leo T and Cetus;

sources.summary: The paper reviews how Eridanus II's nuclear star cluster constrains fuzzy dark matter, contrasting disruption expectations with orbital stripping that can let the cluster survive.

quotes.quote: For example, marsh19 argue that the presence of a nuclear star cluster in Eridanus II implies that m_ 0.6-1 10^ 19 , as for smaller boson masses, the temporal oscillations of the soliton would completely disrupt the fairly diffuse star cluster. However, chiang21 show that irrespective of m_ , the time period of soliton oscillations is much larger than typical orbital timescales within the star cluster, such that it cannot be significantly heated via this mechanism. While the soliton oscillations are adiabatic, schive20 demonstrate that for a boson mass of 8 10^ 23 , the soliton random walk would completely disrupt the star cluster in 1 if Eridanus II were to be located in the field. But, given the galaxy's orbit around the Milky Way, the same study also shows that its outer NFW-like envelope is stripped off with time, greatly reducing the magnitude of the soliton random walk, which is the dominant heating mechanism. Consequently, the Eridanus II star cluster can survive for several Gyr.

sources.summary: The paper discusses Eridanus II as a field ultra-faint dwarf whose distance, backsplash ambiguity, and lack of late star formation limit its quenching diagnostic power.

quotes.quote: The recent discovery of Eridanus II at a distance of 350 kpc from the Milky Way des1, li17 has offered the opportunity to probe the SFH of a ``field'' UFD in significant detail. At a galactocentric distance of 1.2 R_ vir , however, Eri II cannot be considered an isolated system, unaffected by potential environmental effects. A significant fraction of systems at such distances are associated with ``backsplash'' halos teyssier2012, gk14, fham18 , which previously passed within the host's (i.e. Milky Way's) virial radius before returning to the field. While recent observations show no signs of late-time star formation ( li17 , but see also koposov15, crnojevic16 ), Eri II -- as a solitary system with an unknown orbital history -- places limited constraints on the dominant mechanism responsible for suppressing star formation on the smallest scales.

sources.summary: The paper models Eridanus II's halo and central star cluster, finding soliton random walks could disrupt the cluster unless prior Milky Way tidal stripping reduced halo agitation.

quotes.quote: This is significant for the fate of the ancient central star cluster in Eridanus II, as the agitated soliton gravitationally shakes the star cluster in and out of the soliton on a time scale of 100 , so complete tidal disruption of the star cluster can occur within 1 . This destructive effect can be mitigated by tidal stripping of the halo of Eridanus II, thereby reducing the agitation, depending on its orbit around the Milky Way. Our simulations show the Milky Way tide affects the halo much more than the soliton, so the star cluster in Eridanus II can survive for over 5 within the soliton if it formed after significant halo stripping.
The existence of a central star cluster in the ultra-faint dwarf galaxy Eridanus II (Eri II) provides a unique probe for constraining DM models .

sources.summary: The paper describes Eridanus II as the lowest-luminosity Local Group galaxy known to host a globular cluster and cites its cluster survival as evidence for a cored dark matter halo.

quotes.quote: The least-luminous Local Group galaxy known to have a GC is the recently discovered dwarf Eridanus II. This galaxy has M_V = -7.1 and hosts a GC with M_V = -3.5 , which by itself makes up 4 of the luminosity of its host (Koposov et al. 2015; Crnojevi et al. 2016a). Contenta et al. (2017) and Amorisco (2017) argue that the survival of this GC against dynamical friction, despite its projected location close to the galaxy center (offset by only 45 pc), is evidence for a cored dark matter halo in Eridanus II: if a standard cuspy halo were present the GC would have been very unlikely to have survived to the present day.

sources.summary: The paper uses Eridanus II as a dwarf-galaxy benchmark with a measured cored dark-matter profile, an estimated 10^8-10^9 solar-mass halo, and a surviving star cluster.

quotes.quote: Such cored central density distributions ( _ DM (0) constant ) have now been measured in low surface brightness disks Moore1994, Burkert1995, deBlok2001 , and various dwarf satellites of the Milky Way (MW). The latter include, for example, Ursa Minor Kleyna2003UrsaMinorCore , Fornax Goerdt2006Fornax, Walker2011, Cole_2012_fornax, Pascale2018 , NGC 6822 Weldrake2003 and Eridanus II Contenta2018EridanusII .

For example, Eridanus II Bechtol2015 , a MW dwarf (D 410 ) with an estimated halo mass of M_ halo 10^ 8-9 Contenta2018EridanusII also hosts a star cluster Crnojevi2016 . At present, the lowest mass dwarf galaxy known to host a GC is And XXV (satellite of Andromeda, Cusano2016 ), with M_ halo 0.13 10^8 Forbes2018_GC_halo-mass - smaller than the lowest subhalo mass value we use for our tests.

The observations that the GCs in dwarf galaxies like Fornax Goerdt2006Fornax, Walker2011, Cole_2012_fornax and Eridanus II Contenta2018EridanusII are not centrally concentrated, and the fact that they have survived for tens of Gyrs (typical ages of GCs), has been used to argue that these galaxies must have cored DM density distributions. Along the same lines, we expect GC streams in cuspy subhalos to be more significantly disrupted and fluffier than those evolving in cored subhalos.

sources.summary: The paper uses Eridanus II as an observed very low-mass dwarf galaxy with an ancient globular cluster, comparing it to simulated dwarf halos with early cluster formation.

quotes.quote: GCs are present even in galaxies as faint as M_ 10^5 M_ (Eridanus II; bechtol2015,koposov2015,crnojevic2016, simon2021 ), a regime in which standard models of galaxy formation predict that the majority of star formation should have occurred by the end of the reionization era, z 6 bullock2000, benson2002, somerville2002, ricotti2005 .

More broadly, the existence of galaxies such as Eridanus II, which formed 80 of its stars before z=6 and hosts a GC with a stellar population indistinguishable from that of the galaxy simon2021 , demonstrates that GC formation in very low-mass systems at high redshifts ( z > 6 ) is possible and must be accounted for in models. The properties of the GGs and galaxies in our simulated dwarf galaxy halos are in many ways similar to Eridanus II, with GC formation accompanying (or even preceding) the formation of the bulk of the stars in the galaxy, though our systems are somewhat higher in stellar mass.

gmw_00000632.name: Carina
gmw_00000632.overview: Carina is a classical dwarf spheroidal galaxy [arXiv:1204.4612, arXiv:1904.08447] that is tidally disturbed [arXiv:1305.5965] and lacks detected neutral hydrogen gas [arXiv:1809.07831]. It is characterized by a uniquely episodic star-formation history [arXiv:astro-ph/0703308] consisting of multiple distinct bursts [arXiv:1401.6372, arXiv:1509.04929].

sources.summary: The paper uses high-resolution spectra of Carina red giants to revise the dwarf galaxy's iron abundance distribution, finding a metal-poor mean and smaller spread than Calcium-triplet estimates.

quotes.quote: We have performed accurate iron abundance measurements for 44 red giants (RGs) in the Carina dwarf spheroidal (dSph) galaxy. We used archival, high-resolution spectra ( R 38 000 ) collected with UVES at ESO/VLT either in slit mode (5) or in fiber mode (39, FLAMES/GIRAFFE-UVES). The sample is more than a factor of four larger than any previous spectroscopic investigation of stars in dSphs based on high-resolution ( R 38 000 ) spectra. We did not impose the ionization equilibrium between neutral and singly-ionized iron lines. The effective temperatures and the surface gravities were estimated by fitting stellar isochrones in the , \ color-magnitude diagram.

We found that the Carina metallicity distribution based on the corrected \ abundances (44 RGs) has a weighted mean metallicity of =-1.80 and a weighted standard deviation of =0.24 dex. The Carina metallicity distribution based on the \ abundances (27 RGs) gives similar estimates ( =-1.72 , =0.24 dex). The current weighted mean metallicities are slightly more metal poor when compared with similar estimates available in the literature. Furthermore, if we restrict our analysis to stars with the most accurate iron abundances, 20 \ and at least three \ measurements (15 stars), we found that the range in iron abundances covered by Carina RGs ( 1 dex) agrees quite well with similar estimates based on high-resolution spectra. However, it is a factor of two/three smaller than abundance estimates based on the near-infrared Calcium triplet.

sources.summary: The paper models Carina alongside Sextans, using its median orbit and recent stripping history to infer a comparable coronal-density lower limit and test systematic SFH/orbit effects.

quotes.quote: We consider two dwarfs (Sextans and Carina) with well-determined orbits and star formation histories. Our approach introduces several novel features: (i) we use the measured star formation histories of the dwarfs to derive the time at which they last lost their gas, and (via a modified version of the Kennicutt-Schmidt relation) their internal gas density at that time; (ii) we use a large suite of 2D hydrodynamical simulations to model the gas stripping; and (iii) we include supernova feedback tied to the gas content. Despite having very different orbits and star formation histories, we find results for the two dSphs that are in excellent agreement with one another.

Carina We carry out a comparable set of simulations for the Carina dSph. For this dwarf we use only one orbit, i.e. that with median r_ p =51.2 kpc, for which we find =1.7 10^ 4 . Estimating the lower error as before brings the lower limit down to =1.5 10^ 4 (Table tab:simulations ).

Note also that the assumption that there has been no significant redistribution of the stellar component within the dwarf should be fully justified for Carina where t_ lb is only 0.5 . As a final end-to-end test of our systematic error, we consider a pericentric passage at the peak of the SFH of Carina.

sources.summary: The paper treats Carina as a faint Local Group dwarf spheroidal with bursty star formation, modeling its dark halo, gas infall, abundance data, and wind history.

quotes.quote: Carina dSph Carina is located at a distance of 94 5 kpc and belongs to the faintest dSphs of the Local Group ( M_ V 9.3 , _ V =25.5 0.4 mag arcsec ^ 2 , from mateo1998 ). In the dSph realm, Carina is almost unique, since its CMD reveals a bursty SFH, which gave rise to the today observed multiple stellar populations hurley-keller1998,tolstoy2003 . The dataset of chemical abundances we used for the comparisons with the predictions of our models have been taken from the following works: shetrone2003 , koch2008 , lemasle2012 and venn2012 . The observed MDF has been taken from koch2006 .

Chemical evolution of the Carina dSph The most important difference between Sculptor and Carina resides in their SFH. While the Sculptor SFH is characterized by only one episode, with the SFR continuous for 7 Gyr, the Carina dSph has been observed, from the analysis of its CMD, to have undergone a bursty SFH. So, following rizzi2003 and the suggestions of lanfranchi2006b , we adopted for the Carina dSph a SFH characterized by 4 episodes, all lasting 2 Gyr, with the first two occurring from 0 to 4 Gyr and the second two occurring from 7 to 11 Gyr. The galaxy DM halo is characterized by a total mass M_ DM =4.0 10^ 7 M_ , and a core radius r_ DM =0.8 kpc gilmore2007 .

sources.summary: The paper treats Carina dSph as one of four H.E.S.S.-observed dwarf spheroidals in a gamma-ray-line dark matter search, reporting no significant excess and using the galaxies to derive flux and cross-section constraints.

quotes.quote: Data were collected during observations from 2006-2012 for four dwarf galaxies: Fornax dSph, Coma Berenices dSph, Sculptor dSph and Carina dSph. Observations were carried out with the source offset from the centre of the field of view, such that the gamma-ray-like cosmic-ray background flux can be simultaneously measured.

There is no significant deviation from the expected background observed at the locations of the four dSphs. Figure fig1 shows the significance maps for each dSph and in Figure fig2 the significance distributions across the field of view are shown. The fitted significance distributions are compatible with a Gaussian with a centre of zero.

Limits on DM flux The calculated upper limits on the integrated flux in the range 5 _ E of M_ at the 95 confidence level (CL) are shown in Figure figflux as a function of M_ . The differences in flux limits from the four dwarf galaxies arise from the various observation conditions and number of hours of livetime for each galaxy. Constraints on the WIMP cross-section Figure figsigv shows the 95 CL constraints on the velocity weighted WIMP self-annihilation cross-section ( < v> ) versus M_ for the individual dSphs as well as a combined result for the four galaxies.

sources.summary: The paper compares Carina with Draco, giving its distance, mass, half-light radius, maximum velocity, and DGT thermal-bath temperature needed to reproduce its rotation curve.

quotes.quote: Carina and Draco dwarf galaxies A challenge for all gravitational models is to reproduce the rotation curves of the faint dwarf spheroidal (dSph) galaxies from Local Group. We have chosen two of them, Draco and Carina, they are two dSph galaxies, from the Local Group and they are satellite galaxies of the Milky Way galaxy. The Draco and Carina dwarfs are at a distance of 80 Kpc and 100 Kpc from the solar system, respectively. The Carina dwarf has a mass of 4.4 10^5 M_ sun , and this value is twice of Draco's mass 2.2 10^5 M_ sun sawa16 . Fig. draco_rotation summarizes this information and the DGT predictions for the rotation curves of these two dSph galaxies.

The data compiled by give a radius of r_ 1/2 =291 pc for Draco and r_ 1/2 =334 pc for Carina, this suggests that Draco and Carina have approximately the same mass profiles as a function of radius. So, they should have approximately the same velocity profiles. However, according to they have maximum velocities of rotation very different, v_ max =20.5 km s^ 1 for Draco and v_ max =11.4 km s^ 1 for Carine.

sources.summary: The paper describes Carina as a dwarf spheroidal with episodic star formation and finds a nine-star cold substructure near its center using metallicity and velocity cuts.

quotes.quote: The Carina dSph has experienced a complex star formation history. It is the only dSph to exhibit clearly episodic star formation interrupted by long quiescent periods deboer:14,santana:16,hurley:98,monelli:03 . Its color-magnitude diagram shows three different stellar populations corresponding to 11 , 5 , and 0.6 Gyr monelli:03, bono:10 and shows a slight population gradient where the more metal rich population is more centrally concentrated in the galaxy koch:06 . confirmed that the star formation in the Carina dSph is episodic.

We apply the same methods described in Section sec:methods to the Carina dSph. We set the metallicity limits met_ i =-3 dex, met_ f =-0.3 dex and met=0.5 dex. For the velocities we set v_ i =-20 km s ^ 1 and v_ f =20 km s ^ 1 with a v=10 km s ^ 1 . We find a group of nine stars with metallicities in the range 2.89 met 2.44 and velocities 2.93 v 13.31 km s ^ 1 , within elliptical annuli widths of 5 arcmin.

sources.summary: The paper presents Carina as a dwarf spheroidal with deep Araucaria NIR photometry, standard-candle populations, VHS/DENIS coverage, and foreground contamination from its low Galactic latitude.

quotes.quote: This paper includes part I, and presents the data collection for the dwarf spheroidal galaxies Carina, Fornax and Sculptor. While these galaxies are devoid of Classical Cepheids, they do host populations of other standard candles, like RRL and TRGB stars, which have proved useful in precise and reliable distance determinations pietrzynski03,pietrzynski08,pietrzynski09,gorski11,karczmarek15,karczmarek17 . All three galaxies were scanned by the Two Micron All-Sky Survey 2MASS; TWOMASSdb03,TWOMASS06 , and only two of them -- Carina and Sculptor -- by the Deep Near-Infrared Survey of the Southern Sky DENIS; DENIS98,DENISdb05 . Deep and high quality photometry was obtained by the VISTA Hemisphere Survey VHS; VHS13,VHSdb19 for Carina, but not for Fornax nor Sculptor. While the detailed comparison of these surveys and our database can be found in Sec. sec:comparisonsurveys , it is apparent that homogeneous and cohesive deep NIR photometry for all three galaxies has not been available in the literature.

Unlike Fornax and Sculptor, Carina is located at a low galactic latitude of b 22^ , relatively close to the Milky Way's disc. As a result, its color-magnitude diagram (CMD) is heavily contaminated by Milky Way stars, which in turn impedes the statistical analysis of Carina's stellar populations. In this paper we introduce a simple, auxiliary algorithm that finds and marks non-members of the Carina, Sculptor and Fornax galaxies, decreasing the foreground contamination in their CMDs.

sources.summary: The paper finds Carina has a non-negligible v_z gradient at large Galactocentric distance; it may reflect a moderate Milky Way interaction, but orbital uncertainties leave the gradient's origin unresolved.

quotes.quote: Finally, Carina and Fornax (area marked as "III") are the furthermost galaxies, located at 100 kpc of the MW, and both of them show non-negligible gradients in v_z . Carina's positive Galactocentric radial velocity, v_R^ GC = 8.79 0.67 , implies that the galaxy is slowly moving away from its pericentre. Moreover, the median pericentric distance of Carina inferred from the 9 orbit simulations ( d_p = 107^ +1 _ 7 kpc) is compatible with its current Galactocentric distance (d_ GC =106.8 6.3 ) . Given the large pericentric distance of its orbit (the largest in our sample), we expect tidal forces to have a moderate impact on Carina's internal kinematics following a pericentric passage.

The situation of Carina in its orbit is thus unclear. If Carina is in an orbit in which the pericentre and apocentre are close, the interaction with the MW would be unlikely to have caused the gradient due to the low ellipticity of the orbit. Either way, the large variance of the orbital parameters for Carina makes difficult to interpret the origin of its velocity gradient. Further studies on orbital integration may help to shed light on the possible impact of the MW on Carina's internal kinematics.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes the largest detailed sample of CEMP stars in the Carina dSph, finding mostly neutron-capture-enhanced stars plus one CEMP-no star with distinctive light-element abundances.

quotes.quote: We aim to derive detailed abundances of six CEMP stars identified in the Carina dSph and compare the abundances to CEMP stars in other dSph galaxies and the Milky Way halo. This is the largest sample of CEMP stars in a dSph galaxy analysed to date. 1D LTE elemental abundances are derived via equivalent width and spectral synthesis using high-resolution spectra of the six stars, obtained with the MIKE spectrograph at Las Campanas Observatory.

The analysis reveals one of the stars to be a CEMP-no star with very low neutron-capture element abundances. In contrast, the other five stars all show enhancements in neutron-capture elements in addition to their carbon enhancement, classifying them as CEMP- s and - /s stars. The six stars have similar and iron-peak element abundances as other stars in Carina, except for the CEMP-no star, which shows enhancement in Na, Mg, and Si.

We explore the absolute carbon abundances ( A( C) ) of CEMP stars in dSph galaxies and find similar behaviour as is seen for Milky Way halo CEMP stars, but highlight that CEMP- /s stars primarily have very high A( C) values. We also compare the neutron-capture element abundances of the CEMP- /s stars in our sample to recent i process yields, which provide a good match to the derived abundances.

sources.summary: The paper presents FLAMES Ca-triplet metallicities for Carina dSph red giants, finding a broad metallicity spread, radial metallicity variation, and evidence for multiple star-forming episodes.

quotes.quote: We present metallicities for 487 red giants in the Carina dwarf spheroidal (dSph) galaxy that were obtained from FLAMES low-resolution Ca triplet (CaT) spectroscopy. We find a mean [ Fe/H ] of 1.91 dex with an intrinsic dispersion of 0.25 dex, whereas the full spread in metallicities is at least one dex. The analysis of the radial distribution of metallicities reveals that an excess of metal poor stars resides in a region of larger axis distances. These results can constrain evolutionary models and are discussed in the context of chemical evolution in the Carina dSph.

Analyses of the faint Carina dSph have revealed that it contains a variety of stellar populations (e.g., [ 4 ] ), exhibiting prominent old ( > 11 Gyr) and intermediate-age (5--6 and 3 Gyr) populations. This implies that Carina must have undergone several star forming (SF) episodes with at least three significant pulses. Despite this wide spread in ages, its colour-magnitude diagram features a remarkably narrow RGB. Moreover, the overall shape and spatial variations of the metallicity distribution function (MDF) itself contain valuable implications for analysing Carina's unusual SF history.

sources.summary: The paper uses FLAMES red-giant metallicity distributions for Carina and other nearby dSphs, finding their DMD shapes broadly fit simple outflow models while low-metallicity tails require modification.

quotes.quote: Helmi et al. (2006) report observations of several hundred red giant stars in four nearby dSphs (Sculptor, Fornax, Sextans and Carina), obtained with the FLAMES spectrograph in the VLT. The surface [ Fe/H ] ratio of those stars should only marginally be affected by the 1st dredge-up, and thus they may be used as tracers of the chemical evolution of the corresponding galaxies. The resulting DMDs are displayed as histograms in Fig. 2, where they are compared to the simple model with appropriate effective yields ( solid curves ). The effective yield in each case was simply assumed to equal the peak metallicity (Eq. 2). It can be seen that the overall shape of the DMDs is quite well fitted by the simple models.

Before proceeding to the determination of effective yields, we note that the fit of the simple model to the data of dSphs fails in the low metallicity tails: as already noticed in Helmi et al. (2006), there is a lack of low metallicity stars w..t. the simple model predictions. Helmi et al. (2006) attribute this to a pre-enrichment of the gas out of which the dSphs were formed, i.e. they assume that Z_0 0 in Eq. (1) and (2).

sources.summary: The paper describes Carina as a dwarf spheroidal with episodic star-formation bursts, extended quiescent intervals, elevated metallicity for its luminosity, and a reasonable match to model 533.

quotes.quote: This series of well spaced-out SF peaks translate into dispersion in [ Mg/Fe ] at fixed [ Fe/H ] . This spread is more accentuated than for Sextans, due to the extended star formation and the longer intervals between bursts. Fig. FevsL shows that Carina, with [ Fe/H ] =-1.82 and L_ v = 0.72 10^6 M_ , falls above the relation [ Fe/H ] L_ v defined by our models. As already mentioned, we give priority to constraints provided by the spectroscopic data, and allow some flexibility in L_ v .

Under these conditions, model 533 ( M_ i = 2.7 10^8 M_ ) provides a very reasonable fit to Carina's properties. The three major SF episodes are reproduced: 45 of the stars have ages between 9 and 15 Gyr , 29 have 4 to 8 Gyr and 22 are younger than 3 Gyr . Whilst Carina is somewhat less luminous than Sextans, its mean metallicity is higher.

sources.summary: The paper contrasts Carina with Sculptor, describing its subtler population segregation, mild HB/red-clump and metallicity gradients, and more inner, shorter-period orbit.

quotes.quote: We apply these ideas to the Sculptor and Carina dwarf spheroidals. Differences in their orbits might help to explain, under the assumption of similar initial configurations, why in the former a clear kinematical separation between metal poor and metal rich stars is apparent, while in Carina this segregation is significantly more subtle.

On the other hand, weak or no gradients have been found in other satellite galaxies of the Local Group as well, such is the case for Carina, Leo I koch07 , AndIII AndV VI harbeck01 . Carina for example shows a mild segregation between HB and red clump stars, comparable in amplitude to the metallicity gradient found for spectroscopically confirmed Carina red giant stars koch06 . When kinematical information is also available it is found that these stellar populations differences sometimes clearly show up as multiple kinematical components as in the case of Sculptor and Fornax (ref. battaglia06 ). In other cases such as in Carina the kinematical distinction of stars with different metallicities is more subtle.
Orb2 is much more constrained to the inner parts of the Milky Way potential, with pericenters smaller than 30 kpc and orbital periods 1.6 Gyr, almost 2.4 times shorter than estimated for Scl.

sources.summary: The paper models Carina's orbit and star-formation history with observations and N-body simulations, finding bursty star formation, compatible projected dynamics, and an unlikely Magellanic Clouds interaction.

quotes.quote: We present a new study of the evolution of the Carina dwarf galaxy that includes a simultaneous derivation of its orbit and star formation history. The structure of the galaxy is constrained through orbital parameters derived from the observed distance, proper motions, radial velocity and star formation history. The different orbits admitted by the large proper motion errors are investigated in relation to the tidal force exerted by an external potential representing the Milky Way (MW).

Our analysis is performed with the aid of fully consistent N-body simulations that are able to follow the dynamics and the stellar evolution of the dwarf system in order to determine self-consistently the star formation history of Carina. We find a star formation history characterized by several bursts, partially matching the observational expectation. We find also compatible results between dynamical projected quantities and the observational constraints. The possibility of a past interaction between Carina and the Magellanic Clouds is also separately considered and deemed unlikely.

sources.summary: The paper maps Carina with DECam, confirming its population gradients and structural parameters, finding population offsets and only tentative tidal debris that weakens earlier disruption claims.

quotes.quote: We present deep optical photometry from the DECam imager on the 4m Blanco telescope of over 12 deg ^2 around the Carina dwarf spheroidal, with complete coverage out to 1 degree and partial coverage extending out to 2.6 degrees. Using a Poisson-based matched filter analysis to identify stars from each of the three main stellar populations, old, intermediate, and young, we confirm the previously identified radial age gradient, distance, tidal radius, stellar radial profiles, relative stellar population sizes, ellipticity, and position angle. We find an angular offset between the three main elliptical populations of Carina, and find only tentative evidence for tidal debris, suggesting that past tidal interactions could not have significantly influenced the Carina dwarf. We detect stars in the vicinity of, but distinct to, the Carina dwarf, and measure their distance to be 46 2 kpc.

Among these, the Carina dwarf galaxy is one of the best studied due to its interesting star formation history (SFH) and signatures of tidal disruption; some of the main properties of Carina from the literature are summarized in Table tab:main . Carina was first argued to have have an age spread by Mould1983 , and is now known to have an unambiguously episodic SFH (e.g. Smecker-Hane1994 ; Smecker-Hane1996 ; Mighell1997 ; Hurley-Keller1998 ; Monelli2003 ; Koch2006 ; Bono2010 ; Pasetto2010 ). Short bursts of star formation separated by long pauses have created populations of ancient ( > 10 Gyrs), intermediate ( 7 Gyrs), and young ( < 2 Gyrs) stars, as confirmed by the existence of multiple Main-Sequence-Turn-Offs (MSTOs) Hurley-Keller1998 .

sources.summary: The paper analyzes Carina dSph photometry and spectroscopy, separating old and intermediate-age RGB populations with a pseudo-colour index and linking them to different metallicities and chemical evolution.

quotes.quote: We present an analysis of photometric and spectroscopic data of the Carina dSph galaxy, testing a new approach similar to that used to disentangle multiple populations in Galactic globular clusters (GCs). We show that a proper colour combination is able to separate a significant fraction of the red giant branch (RGB) of the two main Carina populations (the old one, 12 Gyr, and the intermediate--age one, 4 8 Gyr). In particular, the \ = (U-B)-(B-I) pseudo-colour allows us to follow the RGB of both populations along a relevant portion of the RGB.

We find that the oldest stars have more negative \ pseudo-colour than intermediate--age ones. We correlate the pseudo-colour of RGB stars with their chemical properties, finding a significant trend between the iron content and the . Stars belonging to the old population are systematically more metal poor ( [ Fe/H ] =-2.32 0.08 dex) than the intermediate--age ones ( [ Fe/H ] =-1.82 0.03 dex). This gives solid evidence on the chemical evolution history of this galaxy, and we have a new diagnostic that can allow us to break the age-metallicity degeneracy of H-burning advanced evolutionary phases.

sources.summary: The paper selects the Carina dSph for detailed generalized J-factor likelihood plots, using a cored dark-matter profile and deriving confidence intervals for self-interacting dark matter parameters.
