sources.summary: The paper presents Sextans A as a very metal-poor dwarf irregular where newly found O stars reveal patchy reddening, outer-region star formation, neutral hydrogen association, and possible isolated massive-star formation.

quotes.quote: The dwarf irregular galaxy Sextans A 10^h 11^m 00.^s8 04^d41^m34^s , dIrr, aka DDO 75 McC12 is interesting in this context because of its very poor metal content. The abundances derived from young H II regions range 12+log(O/H)= 7.49--7.71 SKH89a,Pi01,Kal05,MLC05 and the stellar abundances of and Fe--group elements are similarly low ( [ Fe/H ] , [ Cr/H ] , [ Mg/H ] 1 , KVal04 ). Its poor metal content is also supported by the rather flat UV--continuum of OB-type stars Gal17 . Sextans A is hence remarkable because its population is within the grasp of 8-m telescopes 1.33 Mpc, TRS11 and its 1/10 metallicity is lower than all other Local Group dwarf galaxies targeted by studies of massive stars: IC 1613, WLM, NGC 3109, NGC 6822, IC 10 and the Magellanic Clouds.

The four targets define a new region of star formation far from the optically-brightest centre of the galaxy and from its conspicuous H II shells, but not devoid of neutral hydrogen. In fact, we detect a spatial correlation between OB-stars and H I in Sextans A and other dIrr's that leads us to propose that the neutral phase may be fundamental to star formation in low-density environments. According to the existing evidence at least two of the targets formed in isolation, thus suggestive of an stochastic sampling of the initial mass function that would enable low-mass galaxies like Sextans A to form very massive stars.

sources.summary: DDO 75 is a low-metallicity dwarf target whose maps show two bright regions, with the peak cospatial with other traced emission but narrow north-south extent.

quotes.quote: We present far-infrared spectral line observations of five galaxies from the \ sample: DDO 69, DDO 70, DDO 75, DDO 155, and WLM. While most studies of dwarfs focus on bright systems or starbursts due to observational constraints, our data extend the observed parameter space into the regime of low surface brightness dwarf galaxies with low metallicities and moderate star formation rates. Our targets were observed with \ at the \ 158 m, \ 63 m, \ 88 m, and \ 122 m emission lines using the PACS Spectrometer. These high-resolution maps allow us for the first time to study the far-infrared properties of these systems on the scales of larger star-forming complexes.

DDO 75 has two bright regions visible in the \ map -- one in the middle and a smaller one to the SW, offset by 15 . In each case, the \ peak is cospatial with the , but the central \ knot is relatively thin in the N-S extent ( 10 ), while maintaining the 25 \ E-W extent of the .

sources.summary: The paper describes DDO 75 as the most metal-rich and brightest dwarf in the sample, with possible aperture-edge extended emission in the far-infrared photometry.

quotes.quote: Four dwarf irregular galaxies with properties typical of normal dwarfs were selected for observations with \ for this study: DDO 69, DDO 70, DDO 75, and DDO 210. The photometry sample is slightly different from the spectroscopy sample of - DDO 69, DDO 70, and DDO 75 are common between the two, but DDO 210 has no \ emission line data. They are all nearby (less than 1.3 Mpc) and small (R _D between 0.17 and 0.48 kpc, M_ HI of 7.2 10^ 7 M_ or less), with modest star formation rates of 0.012 M_ yr ^ 1 or less. The most metal rich galaxy in the sample is DDO 75, at 12+log(O/H) = 7.54 (7 Z_ ), while the poorest is DDO 210 at 7.2 (3.2 Z_ ).

Formally, these aperture corrections are for single point source responses, and while they are good approximations for our maps which are not generally dominated by bright extended structure, the flux spread beyond the aperture could be slightly larger than what is accounted for here -- in particular for DDO 75, where there is extended emission near the aperture edge.
DDO 75, the brightest in this sample, has a PACS/MIPS ratio of 0.85 0.54 -- consistent within the error.

sources.summary: The paper measures a high OBe fraction for Sextans A and describes it as the most metal-poor galaxy in the study at about 1.3 Mpc.

quotes.quote: We use a combination of UB , GAIA, Spitzer, and Hubble Space Telescope photometry to identify the complete populations of massive OBe stars -- one hundred to thousands in number -- in five nearby dwarf galaxies. This allows us to derive the galaxy-wide fractions of main sequence stars that are OBe stars ( f_ OBe ), and how it depends on absolute magnitude, mass, and metallicity ( Z ). We find f_ OBe = 0.22 in the Large Magellanic Cloud (0.5 Z_ ), increasing to f_ OBe = 0.31 in the Small Magellanic Cloud (0.2 Z_ ). In the so far unexplored metallicity regime below 0.2 Z_ , in Holmberg I, Holmberg II, and Sextans A, we also obtain high OBe star fractions of 0.27 , 0.27 , and 0.27 , respectively.
Among these three dwarf galaxies, the most metal-poor one that we explore is Sextans A, with a metallicity Z 1/10 Z _ Kaufer04, Garcia17 . It is a distance of about 1.3 Mpc Tammann11 .

sources.summary: The paper applies HST photometry to Sextans A, identifies main-sequence, helium-burning, and OBe-star groups, and finds a high OBe fraction at low metallicity.

quotes.quote: Sextans A, Holmberg I, and Holmberg II are about twenty to a hundred times further away than the Magellanic Clouds. For Sextans A, we cross-correlate Using the Aladin sky atlas F336W and F439W data from with F555W and F814W data from . Before cross-correlating, we shift the data from by 0.6" in right ascension and 1" in declination.

To identify MS, OBe, and HeB stars in Sextans A using HST data, we employ the color-color diagram shown in Fig. fig:coco_sexa . As in Fig. fig:coco_smc , we see that three groups form, which we interpret as HeB, MS, and OBe stars. At 0.1 Z / Z_ 0.2 (i.e., in Sex A, Ho I, Ho II, and the SMC) as much as 30 of the hydrogen-burning sources is an OBe star.

sources.summary: The paper analyzes Sextans A as a nearby very low-metallicity star-forming galaxy, describing its low-surface-brightness environment, interactions, recent star-formation history, tidal tails, and star-forming complexes.

quotes.quote: This paper is dedicated to the analysis of star formation and stellar feedback in the nearby very low-metallicity ( Z 0.07Z_ Kniazev2005, Magrini2005 ) galaxy Sextans A. This low surface brightness galaxy belongs to the NGC 3109 association of dwarf galaxies Tully2006 and is weakly interacting with two nearby galaxies of comparable or higher masses (Sextans B and NGC 3109, separated from Sextans A by 280 kpc and 500 kpc, respectively Bergh1999 . ) However, the whole association is relatively isolated as it is located near the periphery of the Local Group. The most metal-poor massive stars known in the Local Group galaxies have been observed in Sextans A Hosek2014, Garcia2019, Kaufer2004 .

According to the analysis of the star formation history based on the Hubble Space Telescope ( HST ) data, Sextans A has been intensively forming stars during the last 60 Myr, while more quiet star formation activity has been taken place during the last 1-2.5 Gyr Dolphin2003, Dohm2002 . The star formation activity was possibly triggered by the interaction with the other members of the NGC 3109 group, or with the Milky Way, that indeed should happen several Gyr ago and led to the low surface brightness stellar tidal tails at the periphery of the galaxy's disc Bellazzini2014 . The ongoing star formation in Sextans A takes place in 4 complexes clearly distinguishable in far-ultraviolet (FUV; see Fig. fig:map ).

sources.summary: The paper uses Sextans A as a local star-forming dwarf data point, combining H II oxygen abundance, A-supergiant iron abundance, FUV/V-band SFR ranges, and a literature stellar mass.

quotes.quote: Local star forming galaxies Figure fig: data includes the present-day [ O/Fe ] and sSFR estimates for nearby star forming dwarf galaxies: Small and Large Magellanic Clouds (SMC and LMC, respectively), Sextans A, NGC3109.

For Sextans A we adopt the average of the direct method HII region based oxygen abundance estimates from and the average iron abundance determined from 3 A-type supergiant star UVES spectra by . Their uncertainty estimates include systematic errors due to uncertainties in the stellar atmospheric parameters. We use the range spanning between the FUV and V-band SFR estimates from . and the stellar mass reported in Woo08 to estimate its sSFR But note that the Sextans A stellar mass appears particularly uncertain, with values used in the literature ranging from log(M _ * / )=6.24 following , log(M _ * / )=7.38 in , up to log(M _ * / )=8.14 estimated by . Using the latter would result in Sextans A sSFR comparable to that of the MW.

sources.summary: The paper derives present-day stellar abundances for Sextans A from A-type supergiants, finding low iron-group and magnesium abundances, spatial homogeneity, and consistency with nebular oxygen measurements.

quotes.quote: We present the abundance analyses of three isolated A-type supergiant stars in the dwarf irregular galaxy Sextans A (= DDO 75) from high-resolution spectra obtained with Ultraviolet-Visual Echelle Spectrograph (UVES) on the Kueyen telescope (UT2) of the ESO Very Large Telescope (VLT). Detailed model atmosphere analyses have been used to determine the stellar atmospheric parameters and the elemental abundances of the stars. The mean iron group abundance was determined from these three stars to be ( Fe II,Cr II )/ H =-0.99 0.04 0.06 In this paper, we use the standard notation [ X/ H ] = (X/ H )- (X/ H )_ . Averages of the abundances of the three stars are marked with . All abundances are given with two uncertainties: first the line-to-line scatter, second in italics the estimate of the systematic error due to uncertainties in the stellar atmospheric parameters. . This is the first determination of the present-day iron group abundances in Sextans A.

These results are consistent from star-to-star with no significant spatial variations over a length of 0.8 kpc in Sextans A. This supports the nebular abundance studies of dwarf irregular galaxies, where homogeneous oxygen abundances are found throughout, and argues against in situ (``on the spot'') enrichment.

sources.summary: The paper describes Sextans A as one of two nearby dwarf irregular galaxies with similar luminosity, distance, sky position, velocity, and likely shared formation in an isolated dwarf-galaxy subgroup.

quotes.quote: Magrini et al. PNe and H II regions in Sextans A and B \ and \ are both dwarf irregular galaxies (Ir V and Ir IV-V morphological types, respectively, cf. van den Bergh , hereafter vdB00) with approximately the same V luminosity and located at a very similar distance (1.3 Mpc, Dolphin et al. for \ and vdB00 for ). Their separation in the sky is also relatively small ( 10 degrees), which corresponds to about 280 kpc at the adopted distance. Moreover their velocity difference is only 23 6 km s ^ 1 .

All these properties suggest a common formation for these two galaxies, probably together with NGC 3109 and the Antlia galaxy, also at a similar distance and location in the sky. Considering the mean distance of the four galaxies from the barycentre of the Local Group (LG), 1.7 Mpc, this sub-group is located beyond the zero velocity surface of the LG (cf. vdB00) and can be considered the nearest external group of galaxies. Then these galaxies are particularly interesting as they represent a group of dwarf galaxies relatively isolated from giant galaxies.

sources.summary: Sextans A is treated as an original known member of the NGC 3109 dwarf-galaxy association, which the paper finds to be a tight, elongated Local Group structure.

quotes.quote: We re-consider the association of dwarf galaxies around NGC 3109, whose known members were NGC 3109, Antlia, Sextans A, and Sextans B, based on a new updated list of nearby galaxies and the most recent data. We find that the original members of the NGC 3109 association, together with the recently discovered and adjacent dwarf irregular Leo P, form a very tight and elongated configuration in space. All these galaxies lie within 100 kpc of a line that is 1070 kpc long, from one extreme (NGC 3109) to the other (Leo P), and they show a gradient in the Local Group standard of rest velocity with a total amplitude of 43 km s ^ 1 Mpc ^ 1 , and a .m.s. scatter of just 16.8 km s ^ 1 . It is shown that the reported configuration is exceptional given the known dwarf galaxies in the Local Group and its surroundings. We conclude that (a) Leo P is very likely an additional member of the NGC 3109 association, and (b) the association is highly ordered in space and velocity, and it is very elongated, suggesting that it was created by a tidal interaction or it was accreted as a filamentary substructure.

sources.summary: Sextans A is described as a very Fe-poor Local Group irregular with recent intense star formation; OB associations trace current star formation near the H I cavity rim.

quotes.quote: Sextans A (DDO 75, :10:11:00.5 :-04:41:29, J2000.0) is a metal-poor irregular galaxy in the outer part of the Local Group, and the analysis of a sample of young stars has revealed very low Fe abundance (FeII, CrII)/H =-0.99 0.04 0.06 KVT04 , where the first error bar is the standard deviation of the line-to-line measurements, and the second represents the systematic error in the element abundance from the uncertainties of the derived stellar parameters. In fact, Sextans A is the Local Group galaxy where the most Fe-poor young stars have been found until date see Fig. 16, Hosek14 . For this reason, Sextans A offers the unique opportunity to study massive stars in a really metal-poor environment. This galaxy was being forming stars intensely 1-2 Gyr ago, and with a remarkable increase the last 0.6 Gyr Dolphin03b .

We observe a correlation between HI and OB associations similar to the irregular galaxy IC1613, confirming the previous result that the most recent star formation of Sextans A is currently on-going near the rim of the H I cavity.

sources.summary: The paper includes Sextans A among four EMP galaxies, describing it as a 1.4 Mpc dwarf irregular with low oxygen abundance and Herschel observations of its star-forming regions.

quotes.quote: The Sample Our sample of EMP galaxies contains four objects including Sextans A, ESO 146-G14, DDO 68 and Holmberg II (Ho II). Sextans A is a dwarf irregular at 1.4 Mpc with 12+log(O/H)=7.49 based on the direct method Kniazev05 . ESO 146-G14 with a direct method based 12+log(O/H)=7.61 Bergvall95 is a blue low surface brightness galaxy at a distance of 21.4 Mpc. DDO 68 with a direct method based 12+log(O/H)=7.21 Pustilnik05 is a blue compact dwarf at a distance of 5.9 Mpc. Ho II is a Magellanic irregular dwarf at a distance of 3.3 Mpc McCall12 , with a direct method based 12+log(O/H)=7.92 Croxall09 . We also compared our results to the integrated measurements of the KINGFISH sample Kennicutt11 , the DGS (Dwarf Galaxy Survey) sample Madden13 , and other two EMP galaxies (I Zw 18 SBS 0335-052). These properties are listed in Table tab:properties .

Herschel broad-band images (PI: Y. Shi, PID: OT2 yshi 3) of Sextans A, ESO 146-G14 and DDO 68 were taken at 70 and 160 m through the scan map modes of PACS Poglitsch10 , and 250, 350 and 500 m through the small map modes with SPIRE Griffin10 . The exposure time (excluding overheads) in the two PACS bands is 1.9 hr, 1.6 hr and 2.7 hr for Sextans A, ESO 146-G14 and DDO 68, respectively, and in the three SPIRE bands is 6 min, 6 min and 10 min, respectively. The data of Sextans A and ESO 146-G14 have been partly published in Shi14 where the detailed data reduction was given, while the data of DDO 68 is newly presented here.

sources.summary: The paper describes DDO 75/Sextans A as an isolated Local Group galaxy whose central H I disk is dominated by a large Type 1 hole.

quotes.quote: DDO 75 DDO 75, UGCA 205 or Sextans A is located at a distance of 1.3 Mpc from the Milky Way as an isolated member of the Local Group. In V-band light it appears square shaped with magnitude 13.9 . Most of the central portion of the \ disk of this galaxy is dominated by a large Type 1 hole (No. 2). It also has three smaller Type 1 holes, two of which are in the rim of the larger hole, and the last one is in the outskirt of the disk.

sources.summary: Sextans A has an elongated disk-like H I structure with clumps and gaps, gas roughly perpendicular to its optical axis, and a misaligned velocity gradient.

quotes.quote: Sextans A (also known as UGCA 205 or DDO 075) From Ott et al. (2012; their Fig. 13), from the VLA ANGST. The high resolution ( 6 ) H 1 image shows an elongated (PA _ HI 135 ^ ) disk-like structure, with H 1 clumps and gaps. The H 1 morphology is approximately perpendicular to the optical axis (PA _ opt 45 ^ ). The H 1 is enhanced on either side of the optical disk, roughly positionally coincident with the brightest star formation knots, with opposite extensions to the North and South. The velocity gradient direction (PA _ VG 150 ^ ) is slightly misaligned with the H 1 morphology, but roughly perpendicular to the optical disk.

sources.summary: Sextens A is described as having body star clusters apparently not globular clusters, one external possible globular cluster, H I gas, and a location just beyond the Local Group surface.

quotes.quote: Sextens A and B Sextens A has a number of star clusters in its main body but it seems that none of them are GCs 2014A&A...566A..44B . However, one star cluster outside the main body appears to be a bona-fide GC (Beasley 2018, priv. comm.).
Both galaxies reveal HI gas 2012AJ....144....4M so further study of the existing star clusters are warranted. Both galaxies lie just beyond the `zero velocity surface' of the Local Group.

sources.summary: The paper describes Sextans A as a nearby dwarf irregular in the NGC 3109 association and confirms its first known old, metal-poor globular cluster.

quotes.quote: We report the confirmation of an old, metal-poor globular cluster in the nearby dwarf irregular galaxy Sextans A, the first globular cluster known in this galaxy. The cluster, which we designate as Sextans A-GC1, lies some 4.4 arcminutes ( 1.8 kpc) to the SW of the galaxy centre and clearly resolves into stars in sub-arcsecond seeing ground-based imaging.

Sextans A lies in the NGC 3109 association, which is located on the outskirts of (and may or may not be associated with) the Local Group (see e.g., Davidge 2018). The association comprises a loose group of galaxies consisting of NGC 3109, the Antlia dwarf, Sextans A, Sextans B and Leo P (Bellazzini et al. 2013).

sources.summary: The paper describes Sextans A as a Local Group dwarf irregular with stellar and dynamical masses comparable to Sextans B, less-extended H I than its asymmetric stellar light, ongoing star formation, and detected C IV absorption.

quotes.quote: The dwarf irregular galaxies Sextans A and Sextans B are similar in that they have the same luminosity and stellar mass ( M_* 4 10^7 M_ ) and dynamical mass ( 1 10^9 M_ ; Bellaz2014 ). In Sextans A, the H 1 gas is less extended than the asymmetric stellar light distribution and has M( H 1 ) = 6.2 10^7 M_ , which is slightly larger than the stellar mass. In Sextans B, the H 1 and stellar light are nearly identical in size and shape, where M( H 1 ) = 4.1 10^7 M_ , about equal to the stellar mass. Star formation is active in both systems, with similar rates during the past Gyr of 2 10^ 3 M_ yr^ 1 Weisz2011 .
The only detection is C 4 of PG 1011-040 for the Sextans A at heliocentric v=294 km s^ 1 (Fig. fig:SexAspectra ), and 30 km s^ 1 from the systemic velocity of Sextans A.

sources.summary: The paper finds one early K-type RSG in Sextans A, describes a mostly blue-star sample, notes its sparse priority targeting, and identifies one carbon star.

quotes.quote: Sextans A One RSG was identified in Sextans A (SextansA-1906), with an early K-type spectrum, which is compatible with the low metallicity of the galaxy (0.06 ). The sample of Sextans A mainly contained blue stars, the majority of which were classified as O stars.

Excluding Sextans A, which only had 1 priority target, and M83 and NGC 253, which were highly contaminated by H ii regions, the average success rate of our priority system in selecting evolved massive stars is 36 , while for NGC 55, NGC 247, NGC 300 and NGC 3109 the success rate ranged from 38-42 .
Other objects of interest Carbon stars were identified only in nearby galaxies (seven in WLM WLM-1120 corresponds to star 10 in Britavskiy2015 . , one in Sextans A, two in NGC 300).

sources.summary: The paper identifies SextansA as one of the Local Group field dwarfs whose radius-velocity distribution gives a high likelihood of past passage through the Milky Way.

quotes.quote: Comparison of the radius-velocity distributions of VLII populations and measurements of Local Group galaxies presents a strong likelihood that Tucana, Cetus, NGC3109, SextansA, SextansB, Antlia, NGC6822, Phoenix, LeoT, and NGC185 have passed through the Milky Way.

From Table t4 we expect that the following Local Group Objects have with high likelihood ( > 50 per cent) at some point in time passed through the virial radius of the Milky Way: NGC3109, SextansA, SextansB, Antlia, Cetus, Tucana, NGC6822, Phoenix, LeoT, and NGC185.

The separation between the associated and unassociated populations in the distance-velocity plane in VLII was applied in the Local Group to identify field dwarfs that may be associated with the Milky Way: Tucana, Cetus, Antlia, NGC3109, SextansA, SextansB, NGC6822, Phoenix, LeoT and NGC185.

gmw_00000653.name: Holmberg I
gmw_00000653.overview: Holmberg I is a gas-rich dwarf irregular galaxy in the M81 Group [arXiv:2209.04943, arXiv:1912.05352] that features a low-metallicity stellar population [arXiv:2209.04943] and a giant central neutral hydrogen ring [arXiv:astro-ph/0311164, arXiv:astro-ph/0411758]. Its outer rotation curve is strongly distorted, likely due to external gravitational interactions [arXiv:1105.2996].

sources.summary: The paper measures a high OBe fraction for Holmberg I, places it in the M81 Group at 3-4 Mpc, gives a metallicity near 1/6 solar, and identifies 83 bright OBe stars.

quotes.quote: We use a combination of UB , GAIA, Spitzer, and Hubble Space Telescope photometry to identify the complete populations of massive OBe stars -- one hundred to thousands in number -- in five nearby dwarf galaxies. This allows us to derive the galaxy-wide fractions of main sequence stars that are OBe stars ( f_ OBe ), and how it depends on absolute magnitude, mass, and metallicity ( Z ). We find f_ OBe = 0.22 in the Large Magellanic Cloud (0.5 Z_ ), increasing to f_ OBe = 0.31 in the Small Magellanic Cloud (0.2 Z_ ). In the so far unexplored metallicity regime below 0.2 Z_ , in Holmberg I, Holmberg II, and Sextans A, we also obtain high OBe star fractions of 0.27 , 0.27 , and 0.27 , respectively.

The final two dwarf galaxies, Ho I and Ho II, are at distances of 3-4 Mpc Sabbi18 . As such, these objects are beyond the Local Group, and belong to the M81 Group instead Karachentsev02 . According to their oxygen abundances, Ho I and Ho II have metallicities of 1/6 Z_ and 1/7 Z_ , respectively Croxall09, Bergemann21 . In each of these three dwarf galaxies, which are smaller and less massive than the Magellanic Clouds, we identify of the order of one hundred OBe stars brighter than M_ abs = -3 : 62 in Sextans A, 83 in Ho I, and 240 in Ho II (Table tab:nbe ).

sources.summary: The paper studies Holmberg I as a nearby dwarf irregular whose galaxy-sized supergiant shell hosts current star formation, triggered complexes, expanding shells, and progressive shell destruction.

quotes.quote: We present the results of observations of the galaxy Holmberg I carried out at the Russian 6-m telescope in the narrow-band imaging, long-slit spectroscopy, and scanning Fabry-Perot interferometer modes. A detailed analysis of gas kinematics, ionization conditions, and metallicity of star-forming regions in the galaxy is presented.

We show that star formation in the galaxy occurs in large unified complexes rather than in individual giant regions. Evidence of the triggered star formation is observed both on scales of individual complexes and of the whole galaxy. We identified two supernova-remnant candidates and one late-type WN star and analysed their spectrum and surrounding-gas kinematics. We provide arguments indicating that the SGS in Holmberg I is destructing by the influence of star formation occurring on its rims.

Some dIrr galaxies contain a galaxy-sized central supershell that becomes the main feature of the ISM encompassing the whole optical disc. The most striking examples of such galaxy-sized SGSs might be observed in DDO 88 Simpson2005 , Sextans A Skillman1988, vanDyk1998 , M81 dwA Sargent1983, Westpfahl1994 , SagDIG Young1997 , Holmberg I Tully1978, Ott2001 . The latter galaxy is the object of our study in this paper.

sources.summary: The paper says Holmberg I’s rotation curve is strongly distorted beyond 1.5 kpc, likely from external interactions, but the BEC model describes the inner rotation curve well.

quotes.quote: Holmberg I: For >1.5 kpc, the rotation curve of the galaxy Holmberg I shows a severe distortion, probably caused by the presence of strong external gravitational interactions, generating non-circular motions. Therefore the physical parameters and behavior of the galaxy without the presence of external perturbation are difficult to be extracted from the observed data. With the adopted values for the radius and central density, R=2.2 kpc and _c=3.7 10^ 24 g/cm ^3 , the mass of the galaxy is obtained as M_ HOI ^ BEC =0.76 10^ 9 M_ (observed mass M=0.46 10^9M_ ). However, the BEC model gives a very good description of the inner region <1.5 kpc of the rotation curve. The NFW velocity fit fails to give any reasonable description of the rotation curve.

sources.summary: The paper describes DDO 63 as an M81 Group galaxy dominated by a large Type 1 hole and finds seven holes consistent with previous studies.

quotes.quote: DDO 63 DDO 63 (Holmberg I, UGC 5139, PGC 27605) is also a member of the M81 Group, located at a distance of 3.9 Mpc, and dominated by a large Type 1 hole. The first detailed study of the structure was done by who discovered one large hole. Later, found six holes, and our analysis resulted in finding seven holes in the galaxy including the large hole first discovered by . Our estimate of the properties of the holes are in good agreement with both previous studies. The scale height and other properties that depend on the scale height were not estimated for this galaxy due to its face-on ( i = 0^ ) inclination, as discussed in Section ssecbasicprop .

sources.summary: The paper includes Holmberg I among the distant low-metallicity dwarf galaxies, using LEGUS photometry and the same color-color method to estimate high OBe fractions.

quotes.quote: Sextans A, Holmberg I, and Holmberg II are about twenty to a hundred times further away than the Magellanic Clouds. For Holmberg I and Holmberg II we use the photometric data from the Legacy ExtraGalactic UV Survey LEGUS; Sabbi18 . For Holmberg I and Holmberg II, we apply the same strategy as for Sextans A, except that we use from the LEGUS data a V-I color instead of F555W - F814W and U-B instead of F336W - F439W .
We do this for the SMC, LMC, Sextans A, Holmberg I, and Holmberg II. At 0.1 Z / Z_ 0.2 (i.e., in Sex A, Ho I, Ho II, and the SMC) as much as 30 of the hydrogen-burning sources is an OBe star.

sources.summary: The paper models Holmberg I as a dwarf irregular dominated by a giant HI ring, finding that a massive dark halo and roughly 300 supernovae best reproduce its gas morphology.

quotes.quote: We consider two models of Ho I, which differ by an assumed mass of the dark matter halo M_ h . The contrast in HI surface density between the central HI depression and the ring, as well as the lack of gas expansion in the central hole, are better reproduced by the model with a massive halo of M_ h =6.0 10^9 M_ than by that with a small halo of M_ h =4.0 10^8 M_ , implying that Ho I is halo-dominated. Assuming the halo mass of 6.0 10^9 M_ , we determine the mechanical energy required to form the observed ring equal to (3.0 0.5) 10^ 53 ergs, equivalent 300 50 Type II supernovae. The inclination of Ho I is constrained to 15^ 20^ by comparing the modelled HI spectrum and channel maps with those observed.

However, the HI ring in Ho I shows evidence for a supernova-driven origin, since the B-band image of Ho I indicates that there is a pronounced concentration of young blue stars within the ring (Ott et al. ), and the most massive stars of this population might have been a plausible source of energy input over the past 50 Myr in our model.

sources.summary: DDO 63 is described as an HI ring with a central minimum, misaligned kinematic and HI axes, rotation comparable to velocity dispersion, and an XV map dominated by the ring.

quotes.quote: DDO 63: The HI is concentrated in a ring with a high column density, with a five times lower central minimum ( N_ HI = 2.4 10^ 20 2 ) at = 9^h36^m 3^s.1 = 71^ 24'44'' . The kinematic and HI major axes are misaligned by about 30^ . The rotation velocity is comparable to the HI velocity dispersion. The ring dominates the XV map.

sources.summary: The paper models Holmberg I as a dwarf irregular whose giant H I ring is best reproduced by multiple supernovae, not a hypernova or high-velocity cloud impact.

quotes.quote: Only multiple SNe can reproduce both the size (diameter 1.7 kpc) and the ring-to-center contrast ( K 15-20 ) of the HI ring in Ho I. High velocity clouds create HI rings that are much smaller in size ( 0.8 kpc) and contrast ( K 4.5 ) than seen in Ho I. We construct model position-velocity (pV) diagrams and find that they can be used to distinguish among different HI ring formation mechanisms. The observed pV-diagrams of Ho I (Ott et al. ) are best reproduced by multiple SNe. We conclude that the giant HI ring in Ho I is most probably formed by multiple SNe.

sources.summary: The paper treats UGC 5139 as a nearby dwarf in the LEGUS sample, with low extinction and whole-galaxy dust-to-gas analysis.

quotes.quote: We present a study of the dust--to--gas ratios in five nearby galaxies NGC 628 (M74), NGC 6503, NGC 7793, UGC 5139 (Holmberg I), and UGC 4305 (Holmberg II).
UGC 4305 and UGC 5139 are dwarfs.

For NGC 6503, UGC 4305, and UGC 5139, we calculate the dust--to--gas ratio for the whole galaxy, since there are either no published metallicity gradients (NGC 6503 and UGC 5139) or there is not enough coverage in the extinction map to effectively bin the galaxy into radial bins (UGC 4305).

sources.summary: DDO63 is described as a gas-rich dwarf in LITTLE THINGS, with high H I column densities and no detected molecular gas.

quotes.quote: These studies show that large column densities of H i exist in regions where there is hardly any stellar disk mass, good examples being the gas rich dwarfs DDO63 and DDO46 in the LITTLE THINGS survey hunter.etal.2012 .

The remaining 3 galaxies, DDO46, DDO63 and DDO187 are gas rich dwarfs and are all part of the Local Irregulars That Trace Luminosity Extremes; The H i Nearby Galaxy Survey ( LITTLE THINGS ) survey hunter.etal.2012 which is a survey of nearby, gas rich irregular/dwarf galaxies.
Molecular gas is not detected in DDO63 leroy.etal.2009 , DDO46 or DDO187 taylor.etal.1998 .

gmw_00000654.name: Holmberg II
gmw_00000654.overview: Holmberg II is a Magellanic dwarf irregular galaxy located in the outskirts of the M81 group [arXiv:2405.13499]. It features a thick, gas-rich neutral hydrogen disk [arXiv:1103.4494, arXiv:2303.05104] that is heavily populated with expanding H I holes and supershells [arXiv:1512.08820, arXiv:2006.01735], which host active star-forming complexes along their rims [arXiv:1512.08820, arXiv:1609.00650].

sources.summary: The paper constrains Holmberg II's H I disk inclination, arguing that a mean value near 27 degrees restores normal gravitational stability, places it on the baryonic Tully-Fisher relation, and remains MOND-consistent.

quotes.quote: We provide constraints on the inclination angle of the H i disk of the dwarf irregular galaxy Holmberg II (Ho II) from stability analysis of the outer gaseous disk. We point out that a mean inclination angle of 27^ and thus a flat circular velocity of 60 km s ^ 1 , is required to have a level of gravitational stability similar to that found in other galaxies. Adopting this inclination angle, we find that Ho II lies on the right location in the baryonic Tully-Fisher relation. Moreover, for this inclination, its rotation curve is consistent with MOND. However, the corresponding analysis of the stability under MOND indicates that this galaxy could be problematic for MOND because its outer parts are marginally unstable in this gravity theory.
We have shown that, under Newtonian gravity, this occurs for a mean inclination of 27^ . This inclination puts Ho II back into the baryonic Tully-Fisher relation.
All the above arguments indicate that Ho II will be back to normality provided that the mean inclination angle is 27^ .

sources.summary: The paper presents Holmberg II as an irregular galaxy rich in neutral-gas holes, highlighting SGS 17 as the largest old supershell with H-alpha star-forming complexes on its rim.

quotes.quote: Holmberg II is another example of irregular galaxies that have a non-uniform gas structure with a large amount of shells and holes in the neutral gas distribution (see Fig.  fig:hoii ). puche92 found 51 giant holes and supershells in Holmberg II galaxy; bagetakos11 revealed 39 neutral gas cavities using more strong criteria. Their sizes are from 0.26 to 2.11 kpc and expansion velocity -- from 7 to 20 km/s. These values correspond to kinematic age from 10 to 150 Myr. It is necessary to have energy input up to 10^ 53 erg for the formation of several SGS.

The object of particular interest is the SGS 17 according to the list of bagetakos11 - the largest and oldest HI supershell in the galaxy. Optical images in the H line reveal the brightest complexes of star formation located in the rim of this SGS. This supershell is much older than the SGS in IC 2574 described above (150 Myr against 14 Myr). Nevertheless, in both cases we observed that the triggered star formation occurred in the walls of SGS.

sources.summary: The paper measures a high OBe fraction for Holmberg II, places it in the M81 Group at 3-4 Mpc, gives a metallicity near 1/7 solar, and identifies 240 bright OBe stars.

quotes.quote: We use a combination of UB , GAIA, Spitzer, and Hubble Space Telescope photometry to identify the complete populations of massive OBe stars -- one hundred to thousands in number -- in five nearby dwarf galaxies. This allows us to derive the galaxy-wide fractions of main sequence stars that are OBe stars ( f_ OBe ), and how it depends on absolute magnitude, mass, and metallicity ( Z ). We find f_ OBe = 0.22 in the Large Magellanic Cloud (0.5 Z_ ), increasing to f_ OBe = 0.31 in the Small Magellanic Cloud (0.2 Z_ ). In the so far unexplored metallicity regime below 0.2 Z_ , in Holmberg I, Holmberg II, and Sextans A, we also obtain high OBe star fractions of 0.27 , 0.27 , and 0.27 , respectively.

The final two dwarf galaxies, Ho I and Ho II, are at distances of 3-4 Mpc Sabbi18 . As such, these objects are beyond the Local Group, and belong to the M81 Group instead Karachentsev02 . According to their oxygen abundances, Ho I and Ho II have metallicities of 1/6 Z_ and 1/7 Z_ , respectively Croxall09, Bergemann21 . In each of these three dwarf galaxies, which are smaller and less massive than the Magellanic Clouds, we identify of the order of one hundred OBe stars brighter than M_ abs = -3 : 62 in Sextans A, 83 in Ho I, and 240 in Ho II (Table tab:nbe ).

sources.summary: The paper describes Holmberg II as an M81-group dwarf hosting a luminous ULX and an X-ray-ionized Foot Nebula whose radial velocity confirms the source lies in the galaxy.

quotes.quote: An even more outstanding demonstation of X-ray ionization is furnished by the interstellar environment of the L _ x 10^ 40 erg/s X-ray source in the M 81 group dwarf galaxy Holmberg II. As for the other ULX cases the high luminosity is derived from the observed flux presuming galaxian membership, and furthermore assuming that the source emits in an isotropic fashion. To the best of our knowledge this source has first been discussed in the thesis works of mwpakull-E3:d95 and of mwpakull-E3:fou98 . A more recent account can be found in mwpakull-E3:zgw99 .

A narrow slit spectrum (Fig. mwpakull-E3_fig:fig7 ) of the Heel taken under more moderate seeing conditions(FWHM=2 0) is that of a high-excitation H ii region typical for low metallicity (Z/Z _ 0.1) starforming regions, except for the strengths of the [ O I ] 6300 and particularly 4686 emission which are truely outstanding. We therefore propose that we are observing the optical manifestation of an X-ray ionized nebula (XIN), most probably somewhat diluted by more conventional ionization by O stars. The radial velocity agrees with that of the other nearby H ii regions in Holmberg II and proves that the X-ray source is indeed situated within that galaxy. Compared to XIN N159F around the black hole candidate LMC X-1 (.f. Sect. mwpakull-E3_sec:prosp ) Holmberg II X-1 is 30 more luminous, both in X-rays and in He II 4686 emission.

sources.summary: The paper presents Holmberg II as a ULX-hosting galaxy with archival VLA imaging, extended radio emission, measured fluxes, a long inferred source lifetime, and an associated optical nebula.

quotes.quote: We present here the results for three galaxies which exemplify the diversity of intrinsic non-nuclear source types: Holmberg II, NGC 3877, and NGC 4631. The archive included fairly deep 4.86 GHz imaging for each galaxy, with Holmberg II and NGC 3877 also possessing useful 1.4 GHz data and NGC 4631 including a deep 8.46 GHz observation. Fluxes and positional centers for detected radio emission were evaluated by fitting Gaussians.

Holmberg II The radio emission associated with the ULX in Holmberg II is extended, with a deconvolved size of 3^ 2^ . The fluxes are S_ 1.4GHz = 0.98 0.23 mJy and S_ 4.86GHz = 0.70 0.19 mJy, translating to a spectral index of = -0.27 0.36 (under the assumption that the source has not varied over the time of the radio observations, and where S_ ^ ). The implied source lifetime for either thermal bremsstrahlung or synchrotron (under equipartition) is of order 10^8 years. The ratio of radio to X-ray flux, R_X L_ / L_X where = 5 GHz; see neff03 , is about 6 10^ 6 , much lower than that of starforming regions and supernova remnants and consistent with sources such as microquasars. It is interesting to note that Holmberg II is associated with an optical nebula pakull03 .

sources.summary: The paper uses Holmberg II as a dwarf irregular test case, noting clumpy H I, no coherent pattern, and pattern-speed results that differ from the material speed.
sources.paper_coordinate_text: Parameter&Holmberg II& IC 2574 [ 7.5 pt ] Kinematic center R. A. (J2000) &08 ^ h 34 ^ m 06.5 ^ s 8.25 & 10 ^ h 28 ^ m 27.7 ^ s 7.5 Kinematic center DEC. (J2000) & + 70 43 24 12.45 & + 68 24 59 15

quotes.quote: To explore this possibility, a test was performed by applying the solution method to Holmberg II and IC 2574. Both are dwarf irregular galaxies (RC3) that have no coherent or well organized patterns, and show material clumped around expanding H I bubbles (Puche et al. 1992; Walter Brinks 1999). Results for their pattern speeds would represent the averaged angular rotation of the individual clumps of material, and are therefore expected to differ somewhat from .

A. (J2000) &08 ^ h 34 ^ m 06.5 ^ s 8.25 & 10 ^ h 28 ^ m 27.7 ^ s 7.5 Kinematic center DEC. (J2000) & + 70 43 24 12.45 & + 68 24 59 15 Inclination angle & 49 6 & 55 6 Position angle & 275 10 &53 2 V _ sys & 156.5 1 km s ^ 1 &53 1 km s ^ 1 Holmberg II Figure 11 shows the centered and rotated specific intensity map of Holmberg II overlaid with contours of constant v_y . In the figure one can see the clumpy distribution of the H I , and the lack of a coherent or well organized pattern.

Conclusions The results for Holmberg II and IC 2574 are two examples of when _p is not very similar to . Unlike plots of the results for NGC 1365, plots of the results for Holmberg II and IC 2574 suggest specific regions of corotation.

sources.summary: Ho II is discussed as an M81-group dIrr with SMC-like properties and cometary H I morphology; its H I aspect ratio matches plasmon models for Mach numbers about 1.5-3.

quotes.quote: Ho II is a dIrr in the M81 group holmberg:50 . Ho II walter:07,oh:11 is very similar to the Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC) stanimirovic:99,stanimirovic:04 in absolute magnitude ( M_ B 16.7 and 16.1 respectively), HI content ( M_ HI 6 10^8 M _ and 4 10^8 M _ respectively) and total mass ( M=2.1 10^ 9 M _ and 2.4 10^ 9 M _ ). The neutral gas centred on Ho II shows a cometary morphology in its outer regions with compressed contours perpendicular to its direction of motion through the IGM of the M81 group, and extended, trailing tail structures on its opposite side bur02,bernard:12 . It has to be noted that Ho II is probably a companion of the outlying spiral NGC 2403 and its subgroup karachentsev:02 , which does not seem to participate in the ongoing interaction of the M81 subgroup.

Let us now compare our plasmon model with the HI maps of Ho II. >From the map of bur02 , using the N_H=6 10^ 19 cm ^ 2 contour (see their Figure 3), we calculate a length-to-width ratio L/W=0.8 0.1 . This value is consistent with the predictions from our plasmon model for any of the pressure distributions that we have studied, for Mach numbers M_w 1.5 3 (see the two frames of Figure 4). This comparison between the HI emission of Ho II and our model is strictly correct only if the motion of Ho II lies on the plane of the sky. From this exercise we conclude that the straightforward comparison of the L/W ratio of Ho II with the (unprojected) values predicted from our plasmon models is probably reasonable, since projection effects become important only for relatively high values of .

sources.summary: The paper maps star-forming complexes in Holmberg II supergiant-shell walls, finds connected ionized and neutral shell structures, and argues supershell collisions likely triggered recent star formation.

quotes.quote: We report a detailed analysis of all regions of current star formation in the walls of the supergiant shell (SGS) in the galaxy Holmberg II based on observations with a scanning Fabry--Perot interferometer at the 6-m SAO RAS telescope. We compare the structure and kinematics of ionized gas with that of atomic hydrogen and with the stellar population of the SGS. Our deep images and archival images taken by the HST demonstrate that current star formation episodes are larger and more complicated than previously thought: they represent unified star-forming complexes with sizes of several hundred pc rather than `chains' of separate bright nebulae in the walls of the SGS.

The fact that we are dealing with unified complexes is evidenced by identified faint shell-like structures of ionized and neutral gas which connect several distinct bright regions. Formation of such complexes is due to the feedback of stars with very inhomogeneous ambient gas in the walls of the SGS. The arguments supporting an idea about the triggering of star formation in SGS by the supershells collision are presented. We also found a faint ionized supershell inside the SGS expanding with a velocity of no greater than 10-15 .

sources.summary: The paper treats Holmberg II as an actively star-forming dwarf with nearly constant recent star formation, mild enhancements, low average density, and no clear signs of close interaction.

quotes.quote: Here we exploit the Legacy ExtraGalactic UV Survey (LEGUS; calzetti15 ) to study at high temporal resolution the most recent past of three actively star-forming dwarf galaxies, NGC 1705, NGC 4449 and Holmberg II (hereafter Ho II, also known as UGC 4305).

This is not surprising because the CMD of Ho II is dominated by MS stars, for which different evolutionary models generally agree. Both solutions are consistent with an almost constant SF activity over the last 180 Myr, with mild enhancements whose intensity is at most 1.5 higher than the 100 Myr-averaged-SFR ( 0.022 M _ / yr; see the green filled arrow on the right side of the plot) . The SFH obtained with MIST models is less constant, with a broad enhancement between 60 and 100 Myr ago. The SFH with PARSEC-COLIBRI models shows a similar recent peak, whereas the general activity is more constant.

It is unclear what triggered the increases in SF, since Ho II is a rather isolated galaxy, at the very edge of the M81 group, almost detached from it. There are no signs of interaction between Ho II and close-by members of the M81 group, Kar 52 and UGC 4483 are located at a projected distance of 30 kpc and 105 kpc, respectively.

sources.summary: The paper studies UGC 4305 as a nearby star-forming dwarf, linking its HI turbulence most strongly to star formation 70-140 Myr ago and describing its gas holes and ionized structures.

quotes.quote: To determine the timescales over which stellar feedback drives turbulence in the ISM, we performed a spatially resolved, multi-wavelength study of the nearby star-forming dwarf galaxy UGC 4305 (aka Holmberg II). As indicators of turbulence on local scales (400 pc), we utilized ionized gas velocity dispersion derived from IFU H observations and atomic gas velocity dispersion and energy surface densities derived from HI synthesis observations with the Very Large Array. These indicators of turbulence were tested against star formation histories over the past 560 Myr derived from Color-Magnitude Diagrams (CMD) using Spearman's rank correlation coefficient. The strongest correlation identified at the 400 pc scale is between measures of HI turbulence and star formation 70-140 Myr ago.

UGC 4305, also known as Holmberg II, is a nearby dwarf galaxy at a distance of 3.38 0.05 Mpc Tully13 , stellar mass of log (M _ /M _ ) = 8.48 McQuinn19 , H derived SFR of -1.44 0.05 log(M _ yr ^ 1 ) , and absolute B-band magnitude of -16.06 0.04 McQuinn19 . UGC 4305 has an intriguing atomic gas distribution with a distinct flocculent spiral structure seen in the central regions and a comet like structure in the outer-most regions Bureau02 . Across its disk are a large number of well documented HI holes ranging in sizes from 100 pc to nearly 2 kpc with expansion velocities from 3 to 18 km/s (e.g., Puche92,Bagetakos11,Pokhrel20 ).

sources.summary: The paper studies Holmberg II as a nearby dwarf irregular with an HI-hole-dominated ISM, finding no diffuse hot gas above ROSAT limits but many X-ray sources tied to past star formation.

quotes.quote: We present a study of the irregular dwarf galaxy Holmberg II based on PSPC observations (total exposure time: 22 ksec). Holmberg II is a nearby (3.2 Mpc), well--studied dwarf irregular galaxy. It is famous for its interstellar medium which is dominated by expanding structures such as HI holes and shells.

We analyzed PSPC data of the dwarf galaxy Holmberg II. The data provide information on faint X--ray emission features down to a 3 unabsorbed limiting flux of F_ X 4 10^ 15 . This corresponds to a luminosity threshold of L_ X 10^ 37 . In total we detected 31 significant X--ray sources located within the \ column density distribution of Ho II.

sources.summary: The paper uses Holmberg II/Ho II as a face-on dwarf system where extended H I holes can probe gas scale height, while their location and spiral-like arrangement challenge supernova or HVC explanations.

quotes.quote: From this point of view, one may expect that the most extended HI holes in face-on galaxies (such as HI holes number 2, 5, 8, 10 and others in Holmberg II, Puche et al. ) whose z velocity reveals such a behaviour at the inner edges can provide us with a direct measure of the local gas scale height. Specifically, scanning the HI radial velocity profiles within a hole with a beam smaller than its size (for the Ho II case, the beam must be less than 4''-6'' ) could help to identify nearly breaking-through shells, the radii of which could then serve as a direct measure of the local gas scale height.

This tendency is obviously seen in Holmberg II (Ho II) where most extended HI holes are located outside the stellar disk (Rhode et al. , Stewart et al. ). Hence, the SN origin of those HI holes in Ho II is questionable. An alternative explanation of the HI hole formation connected with high-velocity HI clouds (HVCs) falling onto the disk seems to be excluded in the case of Ho II, because no HVCs are found nearby. In this case, Efremov et al. ( ) argue that a supernova in the merging event of a compact binary system may be responsible for HI hole creation. However, a distinguishing feature of multiple HI holes on the periphery of Ho II is that they form quite a regular structure resembling a spiral wave (see Stewart et al. ), and seem unlikely to be produced by regularly placed supernovae explosions.

sources.summary: The paper studies Holmberg II as an M81-group dwarf irregular with deep resolved stellar populations, centrally concentrated old stars, a compact stellar component, and a distorted H I cloud likely shaped by ram pressure.

quotes.quote: Here we analyse the stellar outskirts of the dwarf galaxy Holmberg II (hereafter abbreviated as HoII). HoII is a dwarf irregular galaxy in the M81 group that was discovered by hol50 while surveying the galaxies in this group. Due to its location on the near-side of the group ((m M) _0 =27.65, i.e. 3.4 Mpc), as well as its proximity to the Sc spiral galaxy NGC2403 and similar radial velocity, it is usually associated with the NGC2403 subgroup along with three other dwarf irregular galaxies kar02 .

HoII is very similar to the Small Magellanic Cloud (SMC) in terms of absolute magnitude, \ and total mass: M _B 16.7, M _ HI 6 10^8 M_ , and M _ tot = 2.1 10^9 M_ wal07,oh11 compared to M _B 16.1, M _ HI 4 10^8 M_ , and M _ tot = 2.4 10^9 M_ for the SMC sta99,sta04 . bur02 BC02 While HoII has been observed at all wavelengths, to date the only deep resolved stellar populations study comes from HST/ACS observations wei09 which cover a relatively small fraction of the galaxy. The need for deep wider data is especially motivated by the striking morphology of its \ cloud. From deep VLA data, hereafter BC02 bur02 found that the distribution of neutral hydrogen has a cometary appearance -- compressed on one side with a faint extended component on the opposite side -- with the tail pointing away from the centre of the M81 group. bur02 argued that HoII is moving toward the M81 group and that ram pressure from a hot intragroup medium (IGM) is responsible for the \ morphology, although they could not rule out the alternative interpretation of a gravitational interaction between HoII and one of its fainter neighbours.

sources.summary: The paper studies Holmberg II star-forming regions across ultraviolet to far-infrared data, relating SFR ages to PAH, hot-dust, and broader dust-emission properties.

quotes.quote: A detailed photometric study of star-forming regions (SFRs) in the galaxy Holmberg II has been carried out using archival observational data from the far infrared to ultraviolet obtained with the GALEX, Spitzer, and Herschel telescopes. Spectroscopic observations with the 6-m telescope of Special Astrophysical Observatory of the Russian Academy of Sciences are used to estimate ages and metallicities of SFRs. For the first time, the ages of SFRs have been related to their emission parameters in a wide spectral range and with the physical parameters determined by fitting the observed spectra.

This paper presents a detailed study of the dwarf galaxy Holmberg II (Ho II). As this galaxy was included in several large surveys, for example, SINGS (Kennicutt et al. 2003), THINGS (Walter et al. 2008), HERACLES (Leroy et al. 2009), and KINGFISH (Kennicutt et al. 2011), it was thoroughly mapped in many spectral ranges, from X rays to 21 cm. Both Spitzer observational data at 8 and 24 m and longer-wavelength Herschel data, which allow characterizing not only the PAH abundance but also the abundance of larger dust grains are available for the galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper models Ho II with falling H I velocity dispersion, uses its rotation curve and halo parameters, and identifies it as the sample galaxy with a thick H I disk throughout.

quotes.quote: In addition to assuming a constant value, we use a smoothly falling velocity dispersion to model those two galaxies of our sample, Ho II and IC 2574, for which this more complete velocity dispersion information is available tam09 . The dark matter central core densities _ 0 and core radii R_c are taken from blo08 except for Ho II where we used bur02 . These are determined from the observed rotation curves assuming rotational equilibrium, and a dark matter distribution modelled as a pseudo--isothermal halo. The observed rotation curves are taken from blo08 for DDO 154 and NGC 2366, from oh08 for IC 2574 and from bur02 for Ho II.
We note that, with the exception of Ho II which has a thick gas disk throughout, the dwarfs all show H i flaring.

sources.summary: The paper describes Holmberg II as a dwarf galaxy with large velocity dispersions and likely non-circular motions; the BEC model works in its outer region but not inner regions.

quotes.quote: Holmberg II: Rather large velocity dispersions, as compared to what would be expected for a circular rotation velocity only, appear in the rotation curve of the dwarf galaxy Holmberg II. Non-circular motions and strong baryonic gravitational perturbations in the galaxy are the most probably explanation for the pattern of the rotation curve for <5 kpc. With the values of the radius and central density adopted to predict the rotation curve the mass of the galaxy is obtained as M_ HOII ^ BEC =2.76 10^ 9 M_ (observed mass M=2.07 10^9M_ ). The BEC model describes well the outer ( >5 kpc) region of the galaxy, but fails to describe the inner galactic regions, where any realistic model must take into account the presence of supplementary (local) interactions. The NFW fit cannot reproduce consistently the observed data.

sources.summary: The paper describes HoII as an M81-group irregular galaxy whose H I rotation curves imply observed accelerations far below MOND predictions, making it a problematic MOND test case.

quotes.quote: HoII is an irregular galaxy in the M81 group. Careful analyses of H i observations of HoII have been carried out by three independent groups: Puche et al. (1992), Bureau Carignan (2002) and Oh et al. (2011). The H i rotation curves derived by all these authors are very similar. Oh et al. (2011) derived the stellar mass-to-light ratio in K-band, _ ^ K , for HoII. With such a _ ^ K value (which we will refer to as the nominal value), the stellar mass in the disk of HoII is of 1.5 10^ 8 M_ . Given that the total mass in gas is 9 10^ 8 M_ (Bureau Carignan 2002), the total (gas plus stars) baryonic mass in HoII is about 10.5 10^ 8 M_ . With these empirical values, we infer _ obs =2 0.4 at a radial distance of 7 kpc. This is much smaller than the value predicted by MOND, which is about 19 .

sources.summary: The paper describes Holmberg II as an M81-group dwarf irregular resembling the SMC, with many HI-associated X-ray sources and a heavily studied ULX, Holmberg II X-1.

quotes.quote: Holmberg II is a dwarf irregular galaxy that is part of the M81 group and has properties very similar to the Small Magellanic Cloud. kerp06-02 detected 31 X-ray sources located within the H i column density distribution of Holmberg II to a sensitivity of 10^ 37 \ using \ PSPC data. Many studies of Holmberg II have focused on the unique ULX source Holmberg II X-1, located inside the ``Foot nebula'', from radio miller04-05 to the optical abolmasov03-07 and IR berghea01-102, heida06-16 . Many interpretations for the nature of this ULX have been put forth, although there has been general consensus for a 100 \ BH e.g. goad01-06, berghea01-10 . walton06-15 studied the 0.3-25 keV emission from the ULX Holmberg II X-1 with \ observations, finding =8.1 10^ 39 , which is typical for this source.

sources.summary: The paper presents DDO 50 as one of the largest LITTLE THINGS dwarfs and reports 41 H I holes, comparing them with earlier hole catalogs.

quotes.quote: DDO 50 DDO 50 or Holmberg II, also known as UGC 4305, PGC 23324 and VIIZw 223, is one of the largest dwarf galaxies in the LITTLE THINGS sample. It is an M81 Group galaxy with V-band magnitude 16.6 , located at a distance of about 3.4 Mpc. Previous studies of holes in DDO 50 done by detected 51 holes, whereas restricted the number of holes to 39. The location and size of most of those holes are comparable in both papers. Our hole search resulted in 41 holes in DDO 50.

sources.summary: The paper notes DDO 50/Holmberg II has lower adopted SFR than CMD estimates, remains consistent with the VSF law after rescaling, and shows clear HI scale-height flaring.

quotes.quote: We note that the SFR of DDO 50 (a.k.a. Holmberg II) and NGC 2366 in Table tab:sample are a factor 2 lower than the values obtained by using the CMD of resolved stellar populations.
We found that using these modified profiles does not affect our main conclusion, as both DDO 50 and NGC 2366 remain in agreement with the VSF.

For DDO 50, these authors found HWHM 450 pc over the whole disc, which is approximately compatible with our scale height for R 3 kpc. However, our profile shows a clear flaring, despite the large uncertainties, while the scale height found by is practically constant. This discrepancy, which is mainly in the inner regions, can be explained by the differences in the velocity dispersion profile, which is slightly higher than ours for R 3 kpc, and in the mass model of the galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper treats Holmberg II as a distant low-metallicity dwarf galaxy, deriving its OBe-star statistics from LEGUS photometry alongside Sextans A and Holmberg I.

quotes.quote: Sextans A, Holmberg I, and Holmberg II are about twenty to a hundred times further away than the Magellanic Clouds. For Holmberg I and Holmberg II we use the photometric data from the Legacy ExtraGalactic UV Survey LEGUS; Sabbi18 . For Holmberg I and Holmberg II, we apply the same strategy as for Sextans A, except that we use from the LEGUS data a V-I color instead of F555W - F814W and U-B instead of F336W - F439W .
We do this for the SMC, LMC, Sextans A, Holmberg I, and Holmberg II. At 0.1 Z / Z_ 0.2 (i.e., in Sex A, Ho I, Ho II, and the SMC) as much as 30 of the hydrogen-burning sources is an OBe star.

sources.summary: The review cites Holmberg II as a dwarf galaxy whose HI holes often lack embedded clusters, hot gas, or stellar activity, and whose inferred turbulent driving scale is puzzlingly large.

quotes.quote: Rhode et al. (1999) have studied 51 HI holes in the dwarf galaxy Holmberg II (Puche et al. 1992). They show that in 86 of all cases the holes do not show any signature of the presence of an embedded stellar cluster or any sign of ongoing stellar activity. In fact, X-ray observations of Holmberg II show that the HI holes are often devoid of hot gas (Kerp et al. 2002) and therefore probably did not form by the expansion of a hot gas bubble, sweeping up its environment.

This analysis can be used to determine the scale of energy injection into the ISM for observed HI disks. Applying the method to Holmberg II leads to a driving scale of l_ turb 6 kpc which is very puzzling as this scale is much larger than any known energy source in the galaxy.

sources.summary: The paper reanalyzes Holmberg II, arguing that a lower outer inclination raises its rotation velocity and brings it into consistency with MOND and the baryonic Tully-Fisher relation.

quotes.quote: We re-derive the outer rotation curve of Holmberg II, by modelling its HI data cube, and find that its inclination should be closer to face-on than previously derived. This implies that Holmberg II has a higher rotation velocity in its outer parts, which, although not very precisely constrained, is consistent with the MOND prediction.

On the other hand, we investigate the inclination angle of Holmberg II in detail, building model data cubes and comparing them to the observed ones, thereby showing that the previously derived inclination angle was too high, at least in the outer parts (as could also be qualitatively inferred from the axis ratio in the total HI map).
In addition, with the new inclination, Holmberg II now falls on the baryonic Tully-Fisher relation (its baryonic Tully-Fisher velocity is 65 km s ^ 1 ).

sources.summary: UGC 4305 is described as having complex, nearly symmetric spiral-like H I with holes, clumps, arms, a southern optical loop, a southwestern tail, and north-south velocity gradient.

quotes.quote: UGC 4305 (also known as Holmberg II, Arp 268, DDO 050 or VII Zw 223) From Walter et al. (2008; their Fig. 13), from the VLA THINGS. High resolution (6.95 x 6.05 ) H 1 maps show an almost symmetric spiral-like structure, the internal H 1 morphology is complex, displaying holes, clumps and arms. The optical loop appears associated with the brighter H 1 spiral arm in the South, which encloses a prominent H 1 hole. The Southern (fainter) arm extends as a tail to the Southwest. The H 1 velocity gradient runs approximately North-South (PA _ VG 100 ^ ), within 15 ^ of the optical position angle (PA _ opt 115 ^ ).

sources.summary: The paper describes Ho II as a nearby Magellanic irregular dwarf, uses KINGFISH Herschel plus Spitzer and GALEX data, and reports its EMP-region far-infrared color range.

quotes.quote: The Sample Our sample of EMP galaxies contains four objects including Sextans A, ESO 146-G14, DDO 68 and Holmberg II (Ho II). Sextans A is a dwarf irregular at 1.4 Mpc with 12+log(O/H)=7.49 based on the direct method Kniazev05 . ESO 146-G14 with a direct method based 12+log(O/H)=7.61 Bergvall95 is a blue low surface brightness galaxy at a distance of 21.4 Mpc. DDO 68 with a direct method based 12+log(O/H)=7.21 Pustilnik05 is a blue compact dwarf at a distance of 5.9 Mpc. Ho II is a Magellanic irregular dwarf at a distance of 3.3 Mpc McCall12 , with a direct method based 12+log(O/H)=7.92 Croxall09 . We also compared our results to the integrated measurements of the KINGFISH sample Kennicutt11 , the DGS (Dwarf Galaxy Survey) sample Madden13 , and other two EMP galaxies (I Zw 18 SBS 0335-052). These properties are listed in Table tab:properties .

The Herschel data of Ho II was taken from the KINGFISH project Kennicutt11 . In addition to the Herschel data, the Spitzer IR data at 24 m and GALEX far-UV data at 1516 were also retrieved from the archive.

In contrast, four EMP regions in Sextans A have f_ 70 m /f_ 160 m between 0.5 and 1.0, four regions of Ho II span the color range from 0.7 to 2.3, and three regions of ESO 146-G14 show the color from 0.2 to 0.7.

sources.summary: The paper finds DDO 50 is an additional outlier with high stellar mass for its halo mass, but its wiggly rotation curve makes inclination correction difficult.

quotes.quote: We do, however, find an additional outlier, DDO 50, that appears to have a very high M_* for its M_ 200 , even when marginalising over i . As can be seen in Figure fig:rotcurves_bad , however, DDO 50 has an unusual rotation curve with prominent wiggles out to large radii. This makes it challenging to inclination correct. Indeed, from a stability analysis, 2014RMxAA..50..225S argue for an inclination for DDO 50 of i = 27^ that is substantially smaller than the 37^ that we find here (see Table tab:data ). This lower inclination would be sufficient to push DDO 50 onto the \ relation.

sources.summary: The paper places Holmberg2 among lower-mass galaxies with q=0.4-0.5 and among gas-rich dwarf galaxies, with the upper end of the gas-rich q range.

quotes.quote: Figure 2 and Table 2 show that galaxies with more massive stellar masses (M(*) > 10 ^9 M _ ) have q values close to q 1 at R_ 25 and beyond (e.g. NGC 628 and NGC 6946). The exceptions are NGC 4214 and NGC 5236. Of these two galaxies, NGC 4214 is a relatively small galaxy and close to being a dwarf olling.1996 . In fact, based on its rotation velocity, stellar mass, and absolute B-band magnitude values, NGC 4214 has been classified as a dwarf galaxy by 2008AJ....136.2782L . But NGC 5236 is quite massive ( M(*) 6.9 10^ 10 M_ and so its low q value is quite surprising. For the less massive galaxies, a large fraction of which are LSB dwarfs, q is much smaller (Figure 2 and table 2). Six of them have q=0.2-0.3 (Holmberg1, M81dwfA, IC10, DDO75, DDO187 and UGC8833) and 3 of them have q=0.4-0.5 (Holmberg2, F564-V3, DDO46). The exception is IC1613 which has a relatively large q 1 value at R_ 25 .

It is interesting to note that the gas rich galaxies (9 of them) all have oblate halos, with q<0.6 but the stellar dominated galaxies have a range of q values with q 0.2 (very oblate) to q 1.25 (prolate). Perhaps the most important result from this study is that the gas rich galaxies all have oblate halos at R_ 25 (Fig. 3). There are 9 gas rich galaxies that have M(gas)/M(baryon>0.5 and they are all dwarf galaxies : Holmberg1, Holmberg2, M81dwfA, DDO46, DDO53, DDO75, DDO187, F564-V3 and UGC8833. The q varies between 0.11 in DDO187 to 0.55 in Holmberg2. The stellar dominated galaxies, however, have a variety of q values ranging from q=0.21 in IC10 to q=1.25 for NGC5194 at R_ 25 .

sources.summary: The Euclid showcase describes Holmberg II as a Magellanic dwarf irregular with recent star formation, extended young populations, many H I holes/shells, and an ultraluminous X-ray source.

quotes.quote: Holmberg II , a Magellanic dwarf irregular galaxy, was discovered by holmberg50 in the outskirts of the M 81 group of galaxies karach02 . Star-formation activity in Holmberg II has been relatively constant over the past 100--200 Myr, with a recent peak at 10--20 Myr e.g., mcquinn10,cignoni18 . hodge94 identified 82 \ regions in this galaxy, which capture the effects of triggered star formation on local and large scales stewart00,egorov17 . On larger scales, a star count analysis has shown that, unusually, the young stellar populations in Holmberg II have a more extended distribution than its older stars bernard12 .

sources.summary: The paper treats UGC 4305 as a nearby dwarf in the LEGUS sample, with low extinction and limited map coverage for whole-galaxy dust-to-gas analysis.

quotes.quote: We present a study of the dust--to--gas ratios in five nearby galaxies NGC 628 (M74), NGC 6503, NGC 7793, UGC 5139 (Holmberg I), and UGC 4305 (Holmberg II).
UGC 4305 and UGC 5139 are dwarfs.

For NGC 6503, UGC 4305, and UGC 5139, we calculate the dust--to--gas ratio for the whole galaxy, since there are either no published metallicity gradients (NGC 6503 and UGC 5139) or there is not enough coverage in the extinction map to effectively bin the galaxy into radial bins (UGC 4305).

gmw_00000655.name: 2M1134--2103
gmw_00000655.overview: 2M1134--2103 is a quadruply imaged quasar system with a symmetric core-quad image geometry [arXiv:1711.02674]. The system features a faint lensing galaxy [arXiv:2209.03094] that exhibits near-infrared residual lens light consistent with an old stellar population [arXiv:1711.02674].

sources.summary: The paper identifies 2M1134--2103 as a likely quadruply imaged quasar system, with symmetric core-quad image geometry, near-infrared residual lens light, and a strongly sheared lens model.

quotes.quote: We label this object hereafter as 2M1134--2103 (Figure fig:both , left). At a galactic latitude of +38 ^ , it is unlikely that four point sources of so nearly the same magnitude could be a chance superposition. Moreover, the symmetric image positions are characteristic of a ``core quad'' lensing configuration (in the taxonomy of Saha Williams 2003), albeit with a 2:1 axis ratio which suggests a strong shear and/or ellipticity in the lensing potential.

As seen in Figure 2, there is a significant residual in the K_ s image, which we take to be the lensing galaxy. A second fit was carried out, allowing for a fifth point source. The relative position and magnitude for that source are included in Table tab:tab1134o , as are the K_ s magnitudes for the four quasar images. Finally we used all five relative positions to obtain magnitudes from the corresponding VISTA J band image. The derived J-K_ s colour for the putative lens is consistent with that of an old stellar population at z 1 (e.g. Bruzual Charlot 2003).

sources.summary: 2M1134-2103 has a faint lens and bright quasar images; the paper models F160W and F814W data, finding well-aligned mass and light and high external shear.

quotes.quote: 2M1134-2103 This system has a faint lens and very bright quasar images, which makes an exact modeling of the lens light and spectroscopic redshift of the lens difficult to obtain. We performed the modeling in the F160W and F814W bands. The PSF for the F160W band was estimated by using five stars. We chose three stars to build the PSF in the F814W band. The median mass and light centroid align very well with an offset of less than 0.01 \ in x direction and 0.01 \ in y direction.

gmw_00000656.name: 2M1310--1714
gmw_00000656.overview: 2M1310--1714 is a double-galaxy gravitational lens system located at a redshift of 0.293 [arXiv:1711.02674]. The lens galaxy consists of two components that surround a background quasar at redshift 1.975, producing a core-quad image configuration with a radius of 2.9 arcseconds [arXiv:1711.02674].

sources.summary: The paper confirms 2M1310--1714 as a double-galaxy gravitational lens around a z=1.975 quasar, modeling its two lens nuclei and large-radius core-quad image configuration.

quotes.quote: Our second quad system, 2MASXJ13102004-1714578, hereafter 2M1310--1714, comprises a bright lens galaxy surrounded by four quasar images, again forming a ``core quad'' (Figure fig:both , right). Its 2MASS XSC and PS1 positions are in good accord and the PSF--Kron magnitude difference is typical for a galaxy. Hence this discovery was purely serendipitous. In the PS1 survey this source is deblended into five components, four of which correspond to the quasar images and one of which corresponds to the galaxy. The system has small shear, such that the quasar images fall approximately on a circle, with radius 2.9 arcsec.

The spectrum of the galaxy has low signal-to-noise ratio, but after smoothing we confidently identify Ca ii H/K lines, the G-band and the Mg i b feature, placing the lens at z_ G = 0.293. The two components of the lens galaxy cannot be distinguished with these data.

gmw_00000657.name: BCG
gmw_00000657.overview: The brightest central galaxy of the Abell 133 cluster, also known as ESO541-013 or G4Jy 113, is a cD galaxy with an extended envelope tracing out to 200 kpc [arXiv:2002.11074, arXiv:2308.10071]. It hosts a retired radio source and features a 25 kpc-long northeastern H-alpha filament [arXiv:1401.1822, arXiv:2308.10071].

sources.summary: The paper treats ESO541-013 as the Abell 0133 central galaxy, noting extended H emission, a northeastern filament, and two stellar components including a young population.

quotes.quote: ESO541-013 -- Abell 0133 White et al. \ (1997) classified this cluster as a cooling-flow cluster, however B rzan et al. \ (2004) and McCarthy et al. \ (2004) classified it as a non-cooling flow cluster. More recently, McDonald et al. \ (2010) fit a null spectroscopic mass deposition rate to this cluster, whilst according to Hudson et al. \ (2010), Abell 0133 is a strong cool core cluster (based on the cooling time, which, in their analysis, seems to be the most robust indicator of the cool core strength). The classification of a host cluster as a cooling vs. \ a non-cooling core is troublesome, so for our purposes we will just refer to this cluster as having a zero spectroscopically determined mass deposition rate without making further assumptions. The CCG showed extended H emission in McDonald et al. \ (2010). A single thin H filament (coincident with X-ray) extends northeast from the centre of the cluster for 25 kpc. The ^ 2 values of the SSP and CSP fits do not differ significantly, but the multiple component fitting was found to be more consistent (with the properties and number of components derived for the various spatial bins agreeing within the errors) We do not expect dramatic stellar population (in particular age) gradients for these galaxies, see Loubser S a nchez-Blazquez (2012). , and showed that the majority of the bins consisted of two components: a large, very old component (bordering the upper limit of the stellar populations model at 20 Gyr), and a smaller very young component ( < 100 Myr) contributing up to 20 per cent of the light.

sources.summary: In Abell 133, the BCG dominates the cluster center; its extended envelope is traced to 200 kpc and contributes a large fraction of the cluster stellar mass.

quotes.quote: The cluster is dominated by the brightest central galaxy. We show below that the BCG, including its extended envelope, contributes over 30 of the total stellar mass in the cluster within 500 and 50 within 0.5 500 ( subsec:total ).

We derived the stellar mass profile of the brightest cluster galaxy separately, tracing the wings of its light profile to R 200 kpc. The derived projected profiles of both the BCG and other cluster galaxies are fit to analytic functions defined in 3D, and so deprojection is straightforward.

sources.summary: G4Jy 113 is described as a retired radio source in Abell 133 with an H filament, X-ray filament, and relic lobe energized by its central cD galaxy.

quotes.quote: G4Jy 113: is a ``retired'' radio source coba20 with a z =0.0564, and located in the galaxy cluster Abell 133 owen95 . McDonald et al. (2010) observed the presence of a thin H filament toward the northeast, extending 25 kpc, co-spatial with an X-ray filament, and not consistent with a buoyant radio bubble. Radio and X-ray observations of the radio relic revealed that the relic lobe is energized by the central cD galaxy associated with G4Jy 113, rather than by a shock generated in the radio relic fujita02 .

gmw_00000658.name: Mrk 335
gmw_00000658.overview: Mrk 335 is a low-luminosity narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy [arXiv:1109.1848, arXiv:2206.10731] that hosts a supermassive black hole [arXiv:2206.10731]. The active nucleus exhibits strong X-ray and ultraviolet variability [arXiv:2102.00805, arXiv:2103.16793], parsec-scale radio jet structures [arXiv:2210.16764, arXiv:2308.16780], and low circumnuclear star formation [arXiv:0908.0280, arXiv:1909.04924].

sources.summary: The paper describes Mrk 335 as a high-Eddington NLS1 with multiple observations, a remarkably low XMM flux state, possible complex outflows, and variable bolometric corrections.

quotes.quote: Mrk 335 and PG 1211+143 These objects are both NLS1 AGN. We present two observations for each of these objects, separated by almost seven years in the case of Mrk 335 and three years for PG 1211+143. The analysis of the observations used in this study and the previous analysis in VF07 both place them well into the `high' Eddington regime ( _ Edd > 0.1 ), with X-ray spectra which resemble the `soft' states of GBHs. The 20 ks observation of Mrk 335 is part of the Target of Opportunity programme in which XMM observations were obtained contemporaneously with Swift observations. This captures Mrk 335 at a remarkably low flux state 2008arXiv0803.2516G .

For high Eddington objects, it is likely that complex outflows may play a role in blurring our view of the nuclear emission. It is possible to model the spectrum with partial covering or strong reflection due to light bending 2004MNRAS.349.1435M for both of these objects, again making the intrinsic X-ray luminosity difficult to determine. This would naturally explain the large spread seen in the bolometric corrections (a factor 2.9 for Mrk 335; a factor 3.7 for PG 1211+143).

sources.summary: PG0003+199 is presented as a point-like low-luminosity narrow-line Seyfert 1 quasar where strong H emission enabled a photometric reverberation lag and virial black-hole mass estimate.

quotes.quote: Results on PG0003+199 PG0003+199 is essentially a point-like low-luminosity quasar (Narrow Line Seyfert-1) without a bright extended host. The H line is strong and well covered by the narrow-band filter. This makes PG0003+199 a clear-cut test case. Spectrum Fig. fig_spectrum_pg0003 shows the spectrum of PG0003+199.

Light curves and BLR size Fig. fig_lightcurve_pg0003 depicts the light curves of PG0003+199. The AGN continuum as traced by the B band increases gradually from August 2009 to a peak around end of September, followed by a steep trough by 20 at MJD 55118 (= 55045 + 73). Also the H light curve has a steep 10 trough with a delay of about 20 days. We used the discrete correlation function (DCF, Edelson Krolik 1988) to cross correlate the H and B band light curves.

sources.summary: The paper studies the NLS1 galaxy 335 in low- and high-flux X-ray states, favoring blurred reflection and a compact low-state corona over extreme partial-covering absorption.

quotes.quote: We report on the deepest X-ray observation of the narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy 335 \ in the low-flux state obtained with . The data are compared to a 2006 high-flux \ observation when the source was 10 brighter. Describing the two flux levels self-consistently with partial covering models would require extreme circumstances, as the source would be subject to negligible absorption during the bright state and ninety-five per cent covering with near Compton-thick material when dim. Blurred reflection from an accretion disc around a nearly maximum spinning black hole ( a>0.91 , with preference for a spin parameter as high as 0.995 ) appears more likely and is consistent with the long-term and rapid variability.

The narrow-line Seyfert 1 galaxy (NLS1), 335 ( z=0.025785 ), has invited much interest in the last decade. Historically it has been known as one of the brightest X-ray sources in the sky even detected with UHURU (Tananbaum 1978), but its departure into an x-ray weak and consistent low or moderate flux state (e.g Grupe 2008) marks what may be considered the new normal for this active galaxy. Its lowest flux level in recent years was about 1/30 of the previously lowest observed flux (Grupe 2007), but it is regularly only 1/10 the expected X-ray flux given its UV brightness (G12). Despite the extremely diminished X-ray flux, 335 \ remains relatively bright and therefore the source provides a unique opportunity to study the behaviour of AGN at low flux levels with respectable signal-to-noise.

The measured emissivity profiles of the accretion disc during both the high- and low-flux states show that the drop in flux is associated with a collapse in the X-ray emitting corona from an extended jet-like configuration in the case of 335 \ to a confined region within just a few gravitational radii of the black hole.
